3. Be careful to stir your blanc and not let it stick
4. When there is no more moisture and your fat is melted, moisten it with water
5. Add clarified salt
6. Boil it
7. Skim it and use as needed

title: Blanc-manger Chaud (Hot Blancmange)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound of almonds
- 8 bitter almonds
- cream with one quarter more
- sugar

## Directions

1. Blanch 1 pound of almonds with 8 bitter almonds
2. Pound them very finely
3. Arrange them in a casserole
4. Fill another casserole with cream increased by one quarter
5. Boil with sugar
6. Mix the almonds with the boiling cream
7. Pass everything through fine cheesecloth by straining
8. A quarter hour before serving, put the preparation on the fire and turn it like a porridge
9. Reduce it by continuing to stir until it binds and coats a spoon
10. Pour into a serving casserole

title: Blanc-manger Froid (Cold Blancmange)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 1 pound of blanched almonds
- 8 bitter almonds
- cream to fill 12 small pots
- 6 ounces of sugar
- 1.5 sticks of isinglass
- water

## Directions

1. Pound 1 pound of blanched almonds with 8 bitter almonds until very fine
2. Boil cream to fill 12 small pots with 6 ounces of sugar or less if pots are small
3. Mix the almonds with the warm cream
4. Take a fine cloth and pass the mixture through it by twisting only
5. Dissolve 1.5 sticks of isinglass that has been beaten well and broken into small pieces in half a setier of water
6. Let simmer for two hours
7. Pass through cheesecloth and pour with the warm almond preparation
8. Fill the small pots and place on ice or in a cold place if you have time
9. When frozen, serve

title: Blanc-manger Renversé (Molded Blancmange)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Blancmange (as prepared for small pots)
- Fish glue (at least ten sticks, according to the size of the mold)
- Hart's horn or calf's feet jelly (optional)

## Directions

1. Prepare the blancmange as you would for small pots
2. Fill the mold you wish to unmold with it
3. Add fish glue in proportion; at least ten sticks are needed according to the size of the mold
4. Place it on ice and let it freeze well so it can hold together when turned out of the mold
5. You may make it with hart's horn or calf's feet jelly instead

title: Blanquette d'Agneau (Lamb Blanquette)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- a lamb leg
- mushrooms
- velvet sauce
- coarse pepper
- egg yolks
- croutons
- bay leaf
- parsley
- chives
- butter
- flour
- bouillon
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Spit-roast a lamb leg; when cooked and cooled, remove the meat
2. Remove nerves and skin; mince the meat
3. Beat with the knife handle until pieces are of equal thickness and size
4. Cut and round the angles of the minced pieces as much as possible
5. Place the prepared meat in a saucepan
6. Sauté minced mushrooms and add to the meat
7. Pour in four spoonfuls of prepared velvet sauce and a little coarse pepper
8. Just before serving, place the blanquette on fire with a liaison of two egg yolks
9. Serve with croutons around it
10. If you do not have sauce, pass minced mushrooms in butter
11. When it begins to turn to oil, add a spoonful of flour or more according to the quantity of sauce needed
12. Mix with mushrooms and pour in a spoonful of bouillon, a bay leaf, a small bouquet of parsley and chives
13. Reduce the sauce until thick enough for the blanquette
14. Remove mushrooms and place with the minced meat, pass sauce through a sieve over the meat
15. Just before serving, heat the blanquette and add a liaison of two egg yolks
16. Stir well so it thickens and does not break
17. Check seasoning and add lemon juice if desired

title: Blanquette de Dindon (Turkey Blanquette)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Roasted turkey
- Mushrooms
- Butter
- Lemon juice
- Velouté
- Consommé
- Egg yolk

## Directions

1. When the turkey is cooked on the spit and cooled, remove the meat from the breast
2. Slice and flatten the meat with the knife blade, trim the corners, and prepare the white meat as neatly as possible
3. Arrange the meat in a saucepan
4. Turn the mushrooms and cut them into pieces, place them in water and lemon juice to keep them white
5. When you have sufficient mushrooms for your blanquette, put a small piece of butter in a saucepan with a little lemon juice and your mushrooms
6. Sauté them on the fire; when they no longer have water and the butter is in oil, add six spoonfuls of velouté and as much consommé
7. Reduce the whole by half, taking care to skim and degrease your sauce, and place it over the white meat of your turkey in a small saucepan
8. At the moment of serving, place your blanquette on the fire with a liaison of one or two egg yolks, according to how rich your blanquette is, and a piece of good butter the size of a walnut
9. Check that it is well seasoned

title: Blanquette de Poularde (Chicken Blanquette)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- two hens
- blancs (white meat)

## Directions

1. Spit roast two hens
2. When cooked and cooled, remove the whites
3. Slice them as described in the blanquette of turkey
4. Use the same procedure as for blanquette of turkey

title: Blanquette de Veau aux champignons (Veal Blanquette with Mushrooms)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal loin, underside, roasted on the spit
- Mushrooms
- Butter
- Velouté or flour and bouillon
- Consommé
- Egg yolks
- Coarse pepper
- Nutmeg
- Salt
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. Place the underside of veal loin on the spit; when cooked, let it cool.
2. Slice it, beat the pieces with the flat of your knife blade.
3. Cut off the angles and round your pieces as much as possible; try to make them equal.
4. Put them in a casserole as they are cut and trimmed; use only the white meat and remove everything that has the color of the spit.
5. Have mushrooms that you will turn (shape); cut them thick about one line, forming a round shape.
6. Sauté them in butter.
7. When they are sautéed, put them with the meat of your blanquette.
8. Pour into the butter where they cooked four spoonfuls to degrease of velouté, or more if your blanquette is strong, and just over three spoonfuls of consommé.
9. Degrease your sauce, reduce it, and pass it through a sieve over your blanquette.
10. Keep it warm without it boiling.
11. At the moment of serving, add to it a liaison of two egg yolks, or more if your blanquette is strong, a little coarse pepper.
12. Stir gently your blanquette on the edge of the stove.
13. When your liaison is in and when you see that your sauce is thickened, remove it, always stirring.
14. Check if it is of good salt and good taste; then arrange it in a pyramid.
15. Try to ensure there is not too much sauce.

title: Blanquette à la bourgeoise (Bourgeois-style Blanquette)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Butter
- Mushrooms
- Parsley and chives, chopped
- Flour
- Bouillon
- Salt
- Pepper
- Nutmeg, grated
- Sliced veal
- Egg yolks
- Verjuice or vinegar

## Directions

1. Put a piece of butter in a casserole.
2. Add mushrooms if you have them.
3. Pass through your butter chopped parsley and chives.
4. Put a little flour in it.
5. Moisten with a little bouillon, a little salt, pepper, a little grated nutmeg.
6. Put your sliced veal in your sauce.
7. Keep it warm.
8. At the moment of eating, add to it a liaison of three egg yolks, or more according to how strong your blanquette is.
9. When it is thickened, add to it a little verjuice or a dash of vinegar.

title: Blond de Veau (Veal Glaze)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Two calf necks and two calf knuckles
- Four carrots and four onions
- Two spoonfuls of great broth

## Directions

1. Put in a round saucepan two calf necks and two calf knuckles, four carrots and four onions
2. Moisten them with two spoonfuls of great broth
3. Place your saucepan on a good stove
4. When the broth in your saucepan is reduced, put it on a gentle fire so that your veal has time to sweat and the glaze at the bottom of your saucepan does not stick too quickly
5. When the glaze at the bottom of your saucepan is of beautiful color, fill it with great broth
6. Be careful to skim it so that your blond is not cloudy
7. Do not add salt, since your great broth is seasoned

title: Boiled Eggs (Oeufs à la Coque)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Eggs
- Water

## Directions

1. When the water is boiling, add eggs and boil for two minutes.
2. Remove the eggs and cover them for one minute to allow them to finish cooking.
3. Serve in a napkin.

title: Bonne Morue (Good Cod)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Cooked cod
- Butter, a good piece
- Flour, one small tablespoon
- Salt
- Pepper
- Nutmeg, grated, a little
- Parsley, finely chopped and washed
- Cream, three-quarters of a glass of good cream
- Breadcrumbs
- Parmesan, a little
- Butter, melted

## Directions

1. Cook the cod as in the preceding recipe.
2. Cut it into small pieces and remove the bones; place them in a saucepan.
3. Place a good piece of butter in a saucepan; add one small tablespoon of flour, a little salt, pepper, a little grated nutmeg, and a little finely chopped and washed parsley.
4. Mix three-quarters of a glass of good cream with your seasoning.
5. Place your sauce on the fire, stirring constantly until it comes to a boil; it should be more bound or thicker than the preceding one.
6. Pour it over your cod and stir with a wooden spoon so it mixes with the sauce; then let it cool.
7. Glue a correct ring of breadcrumbs cut into small cubes around the edge of your plate, and arrange your cod so it forms a slight dome; smooth it with your knife.
8. Take breadcrumbs in which you place a little parmesan if you wish, and put it on your cod.
9. Have a small brush, dip it in lukewarm butter, and drain it over your breadcrumbs; coat once more.
10. Pour drops of butter a quarter hour before serving.
11. Put your dish on a stand with fire underneath; place a very hot field oven above it to give it a nice color.
12. When you serve, remove the croutons that are around the edge; also remove the butter turned to oil, and put croutons sautéed in butter to replace the others.

title: Boudin (Blood Sausage)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Thirty or forty onions
- Lard or butter
- Three to four pounds of pork fat, diced
- Parsley
- Chives
- Spices
- Salt
- Black pepper
- Four pints of blood
- One pint of cream
- Intestines, cleaned

## Directions

1. Cut thirty or forty onions into dice; remove the head and tail, and let them melt gently in white by passing them over the fire with lard or butter
2. When well melted, add three or four pounds of pork fat also cut into dice; combine with your onions
3. Add parsley and chives, spices, salt, and black pepper, stirring into four pints of blood
4. Add one pint of cream; mix everything together so that your fat does not remain in lumps
5. Have intestines that you will have washed, and funnel your mixture into your intestines; avoid trapping air inside
6. When your intestine is full, tie it at the end to the size you desire
7. Have a pot full of water whose heat is too hot to hold your finger in; place your boudin in it
8. Do not let the water boil, to avoid bursting it: test it; remove it when it begins to be firm, and when pricked it no longer runs blood
9. Then slice your boudin and grill it

title: Boudin Blanc (White Sausage)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Onions
- Pork fat, pounded
- Bread crumbs, dried in milk
- Meat from a roasted fowl, cooked
- Milk
- One chopine of good cream
- Six egg yolks
- Salt
- Spices
- Intestines, cleaned

## Directions

1. Cut onions into very small dice; cook them as the previous ones; add pounded pork fat that you mix with your onions
2. Add bread crumbs that you have made by drying them in milk; take the meat from a roasted fowl that you chop, and that you pound with your bread crumbs, using equal amounts of bread crumbs, fowl, and fat
3. Add one chopine of good cream; thin your fowl mixture; add six egg yolks, salt, and your spices; mix everything together
4. Pour it into your intestines: cook them in milk cut with water; do not let it boil, to avoid your boudin bursting
5. When it is cold, prick it; grill it on an oiled sheet of paper that you place on the grill over a gentle fire: serve this boudin as an appetizer
6. With this method of making boudin, you may use hare, young rabbits, pheasants, partridge, veal, or pork

title: Boudin Blanc à la Bourgeoise (White Sausage in the Bourgeois Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Good milk, one chopine
- Crumb of bread, a good handful
- Half-dozen onions
- Butter, a piece
- Pork belly fat (panne), half a pound, chopped
- Six egg yolks
- Cream, a little more than half a setier
- Fine salt
- Fine spices
- Pork intestines (boyaux), well washed

## Directions

1. Put on the fire a chopine of good milk that you bring to a boil and add a good handful of bread crumbs, pass through a sieve
2. Boil everything together turning often, principally toward the end, until the bread crumb has absorbed all the milk and is well thick
3. Set it to cool
4. Cut half a dozen onions into small dice and cook them on a small fire without coloring them, with a piece of butter
5. After they are removed from the fire, add the bread crumb with six egg yolks and a little more than half a setier of cream
6. Mix everything together, season with fine salt and fine spices
7. Take pork intestines well washed, cut them to the length you wish to make the sausages, fill them only three-quarters full, tie the end
8. When they are all finished, boil water; when it boils vigorously, gently put in the sausages and boil them until they are cooked, which takes only a quarter hour
9. You will know they are cooked by pricking them with a pin, fat will come out
10. Remove them gently with a skimmer, put them in fresh water, let them drain, and grill them in a paper case, then remove them from the case to serve them hot

title: Boudin de Cochon et de Sanglier (Pork and Wild Boar Blood Sausage)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Onion
- Water
- Pork belly fat (panne)
- Blood
- Cream, one-quarter part
- Salt
- Mixed spices
- Intestines (boyaux)

## Directions

1. Chop the onion and cook it with a little water and pork belly fat
2. When well cooked and only fat remains, cut pork belly fat into dice and put it in the pan with the onion
3. Add blood and one-quarter cream, season with fine salt and mixed spices
4. Mix everything together well and stuff into intestines that you have cut beforehand to the length you wish to make the sausages
5. Do not fill them too full for fear they burst while cooking
6. Tie the two ends of each intestine
7. Cook them in boiling water for a quarter hour to cook them
8. To see if they are cooked, draw one out with a skimmer and prick it with a pin; if no more blood comes out, only fat, this is proof they are cooked
9. Set them to cool to grill them when you wish to serve them
10. The same method is practiced for wild boar sausage

title: Boudin de Lapin (Rabbit Pudding)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- three half-setiers of milk
- three onions
- coriander
- parsley
- chives
- thyme
- bay leaf
- basil
- several rabbit livers
- half a pound of panne (pork fat)
- fine salt
- fine spices
- ten egg yolks
- pork intestines, about eight inches long

## Directions

1. Boil the milk with three sliced onions, coriander, parsley, whole chives, thyme, bay leaf, and basil
2. Reduce this milk to one third
3. When reduced and passed through a sieve, add several chopped rabbit livers, panne cut into small squares, a little fine salt, and fine spices
4. Mix in ten egg yolks and heat everything over a small fire, stirring constantly
5. When well mixed and not too hot, stuff this mixture into pork intestines about eight inches long, filling them only two-thirds as they will swell during cooking
6. Cook them in boiling water for a quarter hour
7. Test if cooked by pricking with a pin; if fat comes out, they are cooked
8. Remove them into fresh water and then place on a plate to grill
9. Serve dry as an appetizer

title: Boudin de Lapin à la Sainte-Ménéhould (Rabbit Sausage Sainte-Ménéhould Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Flour
- Rabbit quenelle forcemeat
- Butter
- Sauce à atelet or other bound sauce
- Breadcrumbs
- Warm butter
- Game forcemeat or fumé de gibier
- Velouté or espagnole sauce

## Directions

1. Spread flour on a clean table
2. Place rabbit quenelle forcemeat (see forcemeat recipe) on the flour in a size large enough to represent a piece of sausage
3. Roll it in the flour and place it in the bottom of a buttered casserole
4. Make as many pieces as you judge appropriate, but three or four are sufficient
5. Poach them the same way as the preceding quenelles
6. When they are poached, let them cool
7. Pare them and give them a square, long and flat shape, fifteen lines thick
8. Coat them with a sauce à atelet if you have it, or another bound sauce
9. Dip them in breadcrumbs and then in warm butter
10. Bread them a second time and give them an attractive shape
11. Half an hour before serving, place them on the grill over a gentle blue fire
12. Place a hot camp oven over them to give them color
13. At the moment of serving, arrange them on the dish and sauce with a reduced velouté, game fumé, or espagnole

title: Boudin de Lièvre (Hare Forcemeat)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Hare fillets
- Veal teat, cooked
- Soft breadcrumbs, soaked in bouillon
- Butter

## Directions

1. Remove only the fillets and remove the sinews.
2. Pound your meat, then pass it through a quenelle sieve and gather it well to form a ball.
3. Have a cooked veal teat which you will chop and pound, passing it through a quenelle sieve.
4. Soak soft breadcrumbs in bouillon and press them well in a clean cloth to extract the bouillon.
5. Pound them and pass them through a quenelle sieve.
6. Set your breadcrumbs, teat, and meat separately.
7. Take as much breadcrumb as hare meat and as much teat as these two combined.
8. If you lack teat, use butter instead of breadcrumbs and meat, that is to say twice as much as the meat.
9. Pound it all together.

title: Bouillon de Santé (Health Broth)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Three pounds of sliced beef
- Two pounds of calf knuckle
- One hen
- Four pints of water
- Five carrots
- Three turnips
- Four large onions
- Three cloves
- One white lettuce with a pinch of chervil

## Directions

1. In an earthenware pot, put three pounds of sliced beef, two pounds of calf knuckle, one hen, four pints of water, five carrots, three turnips, four large onions, three cloves, one white lettuce in which you put a pinch of chervil
2. Let everything simmer gently until the meats are cooked
3. Pass the broth through a silk sieve
4. You can make with this broth all sorts of excellent soups and potages

title: Bouillon maigre (Lean Broth)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 20 carrots, cut into slices
- 20 turnips
- 20 onions
- 4 or 5 celery stalks
- 4 whole lettuces
- a handful of chervil tied with twine
- 1 cabbage cut into strips
- a few parsnips, also cut
- 1 pound of butter
- 1 pint of water
- 2 liters of peas
- 4 cloves
- salt and coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Put in a copper pot or casserole the carrots cut into slices, as many turnips, onions, celery stalks, whole lettuces, a handful of chervil tied with twine, cabbage cut into strips, and some parsnips also cut
2. Put in 1 pound of butter
3. Pour 1 pint of water over your vegetables and bring to a boil until they are reduced to glazing, that is, until there is no more water in your pot and your vegetables are just simmering a little with the butter
4. Then fill your pot with water in which you put 2 liters of peas, 4 cloves, salt and coarse pepper enough so that your broth is well seasoned
5. When your pot has boiled for 3 or 4 hours, pass your broth through a silk sieve

title: Braised Calf's Brains (Cervelles de Veau poêlées)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Three calf's brains
- Lard
- Salted water
- Vinegar
- Lemon slices
- Carrots
- Onions
- Cloves
- Bay leaves
- Thyme
- Parsley
- Chives
- Bouillon or salted water

## Directions

1. Remove the thin skin enveloping the brain
2. Remove fibers from the interior
3. After thoroughly cleaning, soak for two hours
4. Prepare a pot of boiling water with a small handful of salt and half a glass of vinegar
5. Blanch the brains for a quarter hour in boiling water
6. Remove and place in cold water
7. Line the bottom of a pot with lard, place brains on top
8. Cover with lard
9. Pour braising liquid over sufficiently for them to cook
10. If you lack braising liquid, cook the brains between lard, lemon slices, half a glass of vinegar, carrots, onions, two cloves, two bay leaves, thyme, parsley and chives
11. Moisten with bouillon or salted water

title: Braised Chicory (Chicorée Blanche Ordinaire en Ragoût)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- white chicory
- butter
- bouillon
- coulis
- flour
- salt
- shallot

## Directions

1. Clean and wash the chicory
2. Boil it for half an hour in water, then remove it to fresh water and press it well
3. Put it to cook with a little butter, bouillon and coulis, if you have it; if not, make a small roux of flour to bind the sauce
4. When it is cooked, season to taste and degrease it
5. Add a little shallot for those who like it

title: Braised Mutton Tails
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Seven mutton tails
- Lard
- Mutton slices
- Carrots
- Onions
- Cloves
- Laurel leaves
- Thyme
- Bouillon
- Spanish sauce (Espagnole) reduced

## Directions

1. Choose seven mutton tails all of the same size and length
2. Put in a pan bacon strips, a few slices of mutton, four carrots, four onions (one studded with two cloves), two laurel leaves, and a little thyme
3. Cover the tails with bacon strips
4. Pour one spoonfull of bouillon
5. Braise them for four hours
6. At the moment of serving, carefully remove them from the braising liquid
7. Drain them
8. Glaze them
9. Serve reduced Spanish sauce on top

title: Brezolles (Thin Veal Layers)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal round, sliced as thinly as possible, one finger wide
- Parsley, chives, shallots, chopped very finely
- Oil or butter
- Fine herbs, chopped
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- Lard barding or white paper
- Half a glass of white wine

## Directions

1. Cut veal round as thinly as possible, one finger wide, enough to garnish the serving dish
2. In a casserole, put a little oil or butter at the bottom with chopped fine herbs, salt, and coarse pepper
3. Arrange a layer of thin veal round on top
4. Repeat, alternating layers of fine herbs, butter or oil, salt, coarse pepper, and veal round until full
5. Cover the top with lard barding or white paper
6. Cover the casserole and cook over very low heat on hot ashes for an hour and a half
7. At the halfway point, add half a glass of white wine
8. When cooked, serve with the degreased cooking sauce

title: Brochet au Court-Bouillon (Pike in Court-Bouillon)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Pike
- Court-bouillon

## Directions

1. Clean the pike without making an opening
2. Tie the head
3. Put it in the fish kettle
4. Pour court-bouillon over it
5. Simmer for an hour or longer if your fish is large
6. If you serve it as a roast, let it cool; arrange a napkin on a platter and place your pike on it, with parsley around it

title: Brochet Sauce à la Portugaise (Pike with Portuguese Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Pike
- Court-bouillon
- Portuguese sauce

## Directions

1. Cook your fish in court-bouillon
2. When it is cooked, carefully remove the scales
3. Put it hot on your platter and mask it with Portuguese sauce; you may also use butter sauce, tomato sauce, or Indian sauce depending on taste

title: Brochets à l'Allemande (Pike German Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Small pike
- Onion slices
- Parsley branches
- Two bay leaves
- Three scallions
- Two cloves
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- One bottle white wine
- Butter
- Flour
- Nutmeg
- Court-bouillon
- Two egg yolks

## Directions

1. Clean your small pike and cut them into sections
2. Put them in a casserole with onion slices, parsley branches, two bay leaves, three scallions, two cloves, salt, coarse pepper, and one bottle of white wine
3. When your fish has simmered for half an hour, remove it; scale it and remove the fins
4. Put your sections in a casserole and pass the court-bouillon through a silk sieve; pour it over them and keep them warm
5. At the moment of serving, drain them and arrange them on your platter
6. Put a good piece of butter in a casserole; add a small spoonful of flour, grated nutmeg, coarse pepper, and half a glass of court-bouillon
7. Put your sauce on the fire, stirring until it boils
8. Have a liaison of two egg yolks ready to pour into your sauce; stir it well and do not let it boil; pass it through a strainer
9. Check that it is well seasoned and mask your fish with it

title: Brown Sauce (Sauce Brune)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- One or two pounds of beef slice (according to the size of the sauce)
- Two or three pounds of veal
- Poultry trimmings
- Some carrots
- Some onions
- Water

## Directions

1. When you do not have grand bouillon of consommé but you have a little meat, mark a great sauce as you can
2. Have one or two pounds of beef slice (according to the size of the sauce) and two or three pounds of veal
3. Add poultry trimmings, whatever you have
4. Put some carrots and some onions
5. Put everything in a casserole
6. Put a full pot-spoonful of water in it
7. Put your meats on a somewhat hot fire
8. When there is almost no more moistening in your casserole, put it on a gentle fire so the glaze at the bottom of your casserole colors without burning
9. When it is a dark blonde and your glaze is brown, moisten it with water if you do not have bouillon
10. Then put in a strong bouquet of parsley and chives and two bay leaves

title: Bécasses en Entrée (Woodcocks as an Entrée Dish)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Woodcocks
- Lard, grated or butter
- Parsley, chopped
- Chives, chopped
- Salt
- Lard for wrapping
- Paper for wrapping

## Directions

1. When plucked and singed, split them down the back to gut them
2. Use all the innards except the gizzard; chop the rest and mix it with grated lard or a piece of butter, chopped parsley and chives, and a little salt
3. Place this forcemeat in the body and sew the opening closed
4. Truss the woodcocks and cook them on the spit wrapped in lard and paper
5. When cooked, serve them with sauce or ragout as with young partridges

title: Béchamel (Béchamel Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 tablespoons velouté
- 3 tablespoons consommé
- 3 pints cream

## Directions

1. Place the velouté in a saucepan
2. Add the consommé
3. Reduce over high heat, stirring constantly, from eleven spoonfuls to five
4. In a separate pot, place the cream on high heat and reduce by half
5. Stir the cream constantly with a wooden spoon and scrape the bottom so it does not stick and does not take on a burnt taste
6. Mix the reduced velouté and cream together
7. Bring to a boil over high heat, stirring constantly so the sauce thickens and does not stick
8. Stir the béchamel for three-quarters of an hour to one hour
9. When the sauce is sufficiently thickened, strain through a sieve
10. The sauce should be a yellowish white

title: Bœuf au Four (Beef Baked in the Oven)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef chuck
- Beef fat
- Lean bacon, cut into small dice
- Parsley
- Chives
- Mushrooms
- Two shallots
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- A glass of brandy
- Four egg yolks
- Lard strips

## Directions

1. Take as much beef chuck as you judge appropriate and mince it with half as much beef fat
2. Put the meat in a casserole with lean bacon cut into small dice, parsley, chives, mushrooms, two shallots, all minced very fine, salt, coarse pepper, a glass of brandy, and four egg yolks; mix everything together well
3. Line a casserole or terrine of the size of your meat with lard strips
4. Put the meat on top, pressed down well
5. Cover with a lid and seal the edges with flour mixed with a little vinegar
6. Bake in the oven for three or four hours
7. If serving hot as an entrée, remove the lard strips and degrease the sauce; for a side dish, let it cool in its cooking liquid

title: Bœuf aux Concombres (Beef with Cucumbers)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef rib
- Cucumbers
- Spanish sauce or light roux and stock

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your beef rib as you would a braised one
2. Place pieces or quarters of cucumber underneath or around it
3. Glaze them and put a reduced Spanish sauce underneath
4. If you do not have sauce, pass the cooking stock through fine cloth, make a light roux, moisten with your stock
5. Make your sauce somewhat thin so you can reduce it
6. Put your pieces of cucumber in your sauce, which must be very reduced, because cucumber always releases water even when well drained
7. For quarters, cook them in a little stock and fat from your beef rib for about half an hour

title: Bœuf en Miroton (Beef in Miroton Sauce)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Cooked beef breast
- Onions
- Pickles or gherkins
- Parsley
- Chives
- Capers
- Anchovies
- A small pinch of garlic
- Salt
- Coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Take beef breast cooked in a pot, drain it when it is cool and tender
2. Cut it into very thin slices
3. In the serving dish, put in the bottom a layer of sliced onions, pickles or gherkins, parsley, chives, capers, anchovies, and a small pinch of garlic, all chopped
4. Season with salt and coarse pepper
5. Arrange your beef slices on top and season them the same way as the bottom layer
6. Cover the dish and put it to boil gently on a stove for half an hour
7. Serve with a light sauce

title: Bœuf à la Royale (Beef Royal Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- beef slice
- coarse lard
- parsley
- chives
- mushrooms
- garlic
- salt
- pepper
- eau-de-vie

## Directions

1. Lard the beef slice with coarse lard mixed with parsley, chives, mushrooms, and a touch of garlic, all chopped, salt, and pepper
2. Cook it at low fire in its own juice
3. Add about one spoonful of eau-de-vie when it is cooked
4. Serve it cold

title: Cabillaud, ou Morue fraîche (Fresh Cod)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one cod
- salted water

## Directions

1. Clean out your cod and wash it
2. Make well salted water, because the fish only takes up what is needed
3. When the water is clear, tie the head of your fish
4. Put it in the fish cooker with the salted water over it
5. Cook it over a very small fire
6. If serving it as a main course, add a cream sauce; if it is for a roast, serve it plain on a platter with a napkin and parsley leaves around it
7. You can also cook it in a court-bouillon like the turbot

title: Caille au Salpicon (Quail with Salpicon)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Quail
- Salpicon

## Directions

1. Cook quail on the spit or in a small brazier, and serve them afterwards with salpicon, which you will find below in the article on ragouts.

title: Cailles au Chasseur (Sautéed Quail, Hunter's Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Quail
- Butter
- Bay leaf
- Salt
- Pepper
- Fine herbs
- Flour
- White wine
- Bouillon

## Directions

1. Clean and singe your quail.
2. Put a piece of butter in a saucepan with your quail inside, a bay leaf, salt, pepper, and a little fine herbs if you have them.
3. Place your quail over a hot fire and sauté them constantly.
4. When they resist under your finger, take a spoonful of flour which you mix with your quail, a half-glass of white wine, and a little bouillon.
5. When your sauce is thickened, remove your quail from the fire; they must not boil.
6. Arrange them on the plate and check that they are well seasoned.

title: Cailles au Fumé de Gibier (Quails with Game Essence)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- quails
- quail fillets
- salt
- coarse pepper
- parsley
- butter
- lemon
- bay leaf
- bacon strips
- white wine
- espagnole sauce
- game essence
- silk tamis
- quail livers
- farce

## Directions

1. Remove the breast bones from your quail from the inside without damaging the outside
2. After cleaning it through the cavity, singe it lightly
3. Take fillets from other quails and season them with a little salt, coarse pepper, a little finely chopped parsley, a quarter pound of butter, and the juice of a lemon
4. Put two in each quail; you need eight quails for an entrée
5. Truss them and round them as well as possible
6. Baste them so they keep their shape while cooking
7. Put a good piece of butter in a saucepan with the juice of a lemon and a bay leaf
8. Stiffen your quails for a moment in your butter
9. Put strips of bacon in a saucepan, your quails on top of them
10. Cover them also with strips of bacon and put a pan moistened with white wine over your quails
11. Cook them over heat for a good half hour
12. Before serving and at the moment of doing so, drain them, remove the trussing strings, and arrange them on your plate
13. Put an espagnole sauce prepared with game essence on top; if you have no sauce, make a light roux and pass through a silk sieve the cooking liquid in which your quails cooked
14. Pour it over your roux and add a spoonful of jus
15. Reduce your sauce by half, skim off the fat and pass it through a sieve, then place it under your quails
16. If your sauce had too much salt, add the juice of half a lemon and a piece of fresh butter the size of a walnut, melting it by stirring into your sauce without putting it on the fire
17. If the fillets are too difficult to obtain for putting inside your quails, you may chop the livers which you will mix with a little farce

title: Cailles au Gratin (Quail au Gratin)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Quail
- Quail livers
- Cooked forcemeat
- Pepper
- Salt
- Aromatic spices
- Velouté
- Lard fat
- Italian sauce

## Directions

1. Bone ten fresh quail and set the livers aside.
2. Pound all the livers with the back of a knife.
3. Have a cooked forcemeat in which you put your pounded livers with a little coarse pepper, very little salt, and a little pounded aromatics.
4. Mix everything together and fill your quail with this forcemeat.
5. Give them their original shape and secure them with a needle and thread.
6. Pour a spoonful of velouté into the rest of your forcemeat and mix well.
7. Spread this on your plat and place it briefly on the fire.
8. Arrange your ten quail on it and cover them with lard fat.
9. A good half hour before serving, put them on a grate with a hot fire underneath.
10. Cover them with a hot field oven.
11. At the moment of serving, remove the fat and sauce them with an Italian sauce.
12. If you have a large hot oven, they will cook even better.
13. You can also put them au gratin without boning them.

title: Cailles aux Choux (Quail with Cabbage)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Quail
- Cabbage

## Directions

1. Cook them as marked in the article on cabbage, with this difference that you will not blanch the quail.

title: Cailles aux Truffes (Quail with Truffles)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Quail
- Truffles
- Lard
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper
- Four spices
- Parsley
- Veal
- Carrot
- Small onions
- Clove
- Bay leaf
- White wine
- Bouillon

## Directions

1. Clean your quail through the cavity and singe them lightly.
2. Peel your truffles and cut them into large dice.
3. Grate a little lard and put it in a saucepan with as much butter, salt, pepper, four spices, and a little parsley.
4. Chop a truffle and mix it with the rest.
5. Pass this over the fire for seven or eight minutes and let it cool.
6. Fill the body of your quail with this mixture.
7. When well filled, truss and bard them and give them an agreeable shape.
8. Put lard fat in a saucepan with your quail on top and cover them with lard fat.
9. Cut small pieces of veal into dice, a carrot, seven or eight small onions, a clove, and half a bay leaf.
10. Add your truffle peelings and a piece of butter.
11. Put all this in a saucepan and cook your seasoning for a good quarter hour over a moderate fire.
12. When your seasoning is well browned, add a glass of white wine and as much bouillon.
13. Let it come to a boil two or three times, then pour this seasoning over your quail when you put them on the fire.
14. A good half hour is sufficient to cook them.
15. At the moment of serving, drain them, unbard them, and arrange them on the plate.
16. In the middle, put a sauté of truffles arranged in a reduced Spanish sauce.

title: Cailles en Gratin (Quail Gratin)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Six or seven quail
- Butter
- Bouquet of parsley
- Chives
- Half a clove of garlic
- Two cloves
- Half a bay leaf
- Thyme
- Basil
- Mushrooms
- Flour
- White wine
- Bouillon
- Salt
- Pepper
- Sweetbread of veal
- Quail livers
- Parsley
- Chives
- Breadcrumbs
- Butter
- Salt
- Pepper
- Egg yolks

## Directions

1. Flame and clean six or seven quail, pass them in a saucepan over the fire with a piece of butter, a bouquet of parsley, chives, half a clove of garlic, two cloves, half a bay leaf, thyme, basil, and mushrooms.
2. Add a good pinch of flour, moisten with white wine, bouillon, and juice as needed to give color, salt, and coarse pepper.
3. At half the cooking time, add a sweetbread of veal blanched and cut into large dice, finish cooking and reduce the sauce to the point of a bound sauce.
4. For the gratin: chop the quail livers with parsley and chives, mix with a little breadcrumbs, a piece of butter, salt, coarse pepper, and two egg yolks.
5. Place this forcemeat in the bottom of the serving dish and put it over a small fire until the forcemeat is gratinéed.
6. Serve the ragout over it.

title: Cailles en Gratin (Quails in Gratin)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Six or seven quails
- Butter
- Bouquet of parsley, chives, half a clove of garlic
- Two cloves
- Half a bay leaf
- Thyme
- Basil
- Mushrooms
- Flour
- White wine
- Bouillon
- Jus

## Directions

1. Pluck and singe the quails, then gut them
2. Pass them through a casserole over the fire with a piece of butter, a bouquet of parsley, chives, half a clove of garlic, two cloves, half a bay leaf, thyme, basil, and mushrooms
3. Sprinkle with a good pinch of flour
4. Moisten with white wine, bouillon, and jus as needed

title: Cailles à l'Espagnole (Quails Spanish Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- eight fresh quails
- butter
- lemon juice
- salt
- coarse pepper
- bacon strips
- needle and coarse thread
- white wine
- bouillon
- croutons
- espagnole sauce

## Directions

1. Have eight very fresh quails that you clean through the cavity to avoid a large incision near the rump
2. Work a piece of butter in which you put lemon juice, salt, and coarse pepper
3. Put as much as will fit in your quails
4. Fasten the thighs with a needle and coarse thread
5. Leave the legs free
6. Give them an agreeable shape by trussing them
7. Put strips of bacon in a saucepan, your quails on top of them
8. Cover them also with strips of bacon and put a pan moistened with half white wine and half bouillon on top
9. A good half hour before serving, put them over heat
10. When removing them, drain them and remove the trussing strings
11. Have croutons the size of your quails and place them on top
12. Put an espagnole sauce

title: Calf's Brains in Aspic (Cervelles de Veau en aspic)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Aspic

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your brains as you would for the panfried version
2. Pour lukewarm aspic into the bottom of a mold to a depth of seven or eight lines
3. When your aspic is cold and set, cut your brains into four pieces and arrange them so that they form a beautiful pattern and are positioned on your jelly to create a design
4. Pour nearly cold aspic over them so that your brains are well enclosed in the jelly
5. Place your mold on ice to allow the aspic to congeal
6. When it is well set, at the moment of serving, heat a cloth well and rub the mold with it until you see your aspic begin to release, or dip the mold in lukewarm water without leaving it in too long for fear the jelly will melt too much
7. Place your platter over your mold and invert it
8. If your aspic does not release, place a warm cloth on your mold and rub it around with this same warm cloth
9. Lift your mold very gently, taking care not to damage your aspic
10. If there is melted jelly on the platter, use a straw to suck it up
11. Place your platter briefly on ice

title: Calf's Brains in Tomato Sauce (Cervelles de Veau à la sauce tomate)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Tomato sauce

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your brains as you would for the panfried version
2. At the moment of service, drain them
3. Arrange them on the platter
4. Top with tomato sauce

title: Calf's Brains Matelote (Cervelles de Veau en matelote)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Lard
- Carrots
- Onions
- Bay leaves
- Parsley
- Chives
- White wine
- Small onions
- Butter
- Roux
- Jus
- Mushrooms
- Bouquet garni
- Salt

## Directions

1. Prepare the brains as directed for braised
2. Cook between lard, two carrots, three onions cut in slices, two bay leaves, parsley and chives bouquet
3. Moisten with white wine
4. Cook for three quarters of an hour
5. When cooked, at moment of serving, drain and arrange on a plate
6. Have small onions well cleaned
7. Sauté them in butter until blonde
8. Transfer to another pot
9. Make a light roux and moisten with white wine and a little jus
10. Pour the sauce over the onions
11. Add mushrooms and bouquet garni
12. Cook everything together
13. Skim and degrease the ragout
14. When cooked, pour over the brains
15. The sauce should be neither too thick nor too thin and properly salted

title: Calf's Brains Maître d'Hôtel (Cervelles de Veau à la maître-d'hôtel)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Fine butter
- Flour
- Ravigote
- Salt
- Pepper
- Tarragon vinegar
- Water

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the brains as directed for braised
2. Drain them
3. Arrange on your plate
4. Have a quarter pound of fine butter in a pot
5. Add three-quarters of a tablespoon of flour and chopped ravigote
6. Blend everything together with a wooden spoon
7. Add salt, coarse pepper, a dash of tarragon vinegar, and a little water
8. Place the pot on the fire and turn the sauce
9. The sauce must be thick enough to coat the brains

title: Calf's Brains with Black Butter (Cervelles de Veau au beurre noir)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Black butter
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the brains as directed for braised
2. When cooked, at moment of serving, drain and arrange on plate
3. Sauce with black butter
4. Place a bouquet of fried parsley in the middle of the brains

title: Calf's Brains with Crayfish Butter (Cervelles de Veau au beurre d'écrevisses)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Reduced velouté
- Crayfish butter

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the brains as directed for braised
2. At moment of serving, drain and arrange on plate
3. Have four skimming-spoonfuls of reduced velouté into which you put the size of a small egg of crayfish butter
4. Stir the sauce well so the butter melts and binds with the sauce

title: Calf's Brains with Crayfish Butter (Cervelles de Veau au beurre d'écrevisses)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Four spoonfuls of reduced velouté
- Crayfish butter, about the size of a small egg
- Coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your brains as you would for the panfried version
2. At the moment of serving, drain them and arrange them on the platter
3. Have ready four spoonfuls of reduced velouté in which you will place crayfish butter about the size of a small egg
4. Stir your sauce well so that the butter melts and blends with your sauce, without allowing it to boil
5. When the butter is incorporated, add a little coarse pepper and pour your sauce over your brains

title: Calf's Brains with Hollandaise Sauce (Cervelles de Veau sauce hollandaise)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Crayfish
- Velouté
- Tarragon vinegar
- Ravigote
- Spinach green
- Bouillon
- Roux
- Pepper
- Salt

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the brains as directed for braised
2. At moment of serving, drain them keeping them whole
3. Place in dish with small end in the middle
4. Place a fine crayfish between them
5. Have reduced velouté into which you put a dash of tarragon vinegar
6. Take blanched ravigote passed through cloth and put in sauce at moment of serving
7. If sauce is not green enough, add a little spinach green
8. If you lack velouté, make a light roux moistened with bouillon
9. Add a dash of tarragon vinegar and coarse pepper
10. Reduce the sauce
11. At moment of serving add finely chopped ravigote with a little spinach green to green the sauce
12. Ensure it is properly salted and of good flavor

title: Calf's Brains with Tomato Sauce (Cervelles de Veau à la sauce tomate)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's brains
- Tomato sauce

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the brains as directed for braised
2. At moment of service drain them
3. Arrange on the plate
4. Top with tomato sauce

title: Calf's Ears in Mushroom Ragout (Oreilles de Veau en ragoût aux champignons)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's ears
- Mushroom ragout
- Butter
- Velouté
- Consommé
- Egg yolks

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook the ears as directed for the Italian style
2. When cooked, drain them and arrange on the plate
3. Top with mushroom ragout
4. Turn and sauté them in butter
5. Add four skimming-spoonfuls of velouté and as much consommé
6. Reduce the sauce by half
7. At moment of serving, bind the ragout with two egg yolks and pour over the ears on the plate

title: Calf's Ears with Marinade (Oreilles de Veau en marinade)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's ears
- Blanc (cooking liquid)
- Marinade

## Directions

1. Prepare the ears as directed for the Italian style
2. Cook them in a blanc
3. When cooked, drain them
4. Cut them lengthwise into two, three or four pieces as you see fit
5. Pour marinade over them a quarter hour before serving
6. Drain them
7. Put them in frying batter
8. Put them in oil that is not too hot
9. When they have a nice color, remove them
10. Drain them on white cloth
11. Arrange them on your plate
12. Fry a handful of parsley and arrange it in a pyramid on top

title: Calf's Feet Fricassee (Pieds de Veau en Fricassée de Poulet)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's feet
- butter
- mushrooms
- parsley
- chives
- garlic clove
- shallots
- bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- cloves
- flour
- white wine
- bouillon
- salt
- pepper
- egg yolks
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Cut the cooked calf's feet into pieces and place them in a casserole with a good piece of butter, mushrooms, a bouquet of parsley, chives, a clove of garlic, two shallots, a bay leaf, thyme, basil, and two cloves
2. Pass them over the fire and add a pinch of flour, moisten with a glass of white wine and as much bouillon
3. Season with salt and coarse pepper and bring to boil for half an hour over low heat
4. When the sauce is reduced by half, remove the bouquet and add three egg yolks mixed with a spoonful of vinegar and as much bouillon
5. Make the sauce bind without boiling and serve

title: Calf's Head Stuffed, Bourgeoise Style (Tête de Veau Farcie à la Bourgeoise)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head with skin, well blanched and scalded
- calf's brains
- veal round
- beef fat
- salt
- coarse pepper
- parsley
- chives
- half a bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- eau-de-vie
- egg yolks
- egg whites
- calf's tongue
- calf's eyes

## Directions

1. Use a calf's head with skin that is well blanched and well scalded.
2. Carefully remove the skin without cutting it.
3. Bone the head to extract the brains, tongue, eyes, and cheeks.
4. Make a forcemeat with the brains, veal round, and beef fat, all chopped very fine.
5. Season with salt, coarse pepper, parsley, chives (chopped), half a bay leaf, thyme and basil (chopped like powder).
6. Add two spoonfuls of eau-de-vie and bind the forcemeat with three egg yolks and three beaten egg whites.
7. Blanch the tongue, eyes (from which you remove all the dark part), and cheeks in boiling water, clean them properly, then cut them into fillets or large dice.
8. Mix these pieces into your forcemeat.
9. Place the calf's head skin, unblanced, in a casserole with the ears underneath, and fill it with your forcemeat.
10. Sew the head closed.

title: Calf's Shoulder in Galantine (Epaule de Veau en galantine)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's shoulder, well covered
- Meat from the shoulder for farce
- Lard, one pound for each pound of meat
- Bacon lardons
- Tongue at scarlet
- Truffles
- Spinach omelette, if desired
- Carrots
- Lard for covering
- Veal lardons, additional
- Vegetables and aromatics for the braising liquid: veal slices, two veal feet blanched, shoulder bones, six carrots, eight to ten onions (one studded with four cloves), four bay leaves, thyme, parsley and chives
- Beef broth or water and salt

## Directions

1. Select a calf's shoulder that is well covered; the top should not be damaged
2. Bone it entirely
3. Remove some of the meat with which you will make your farce, if you do not have a slice of veal
4. For every pound of meat, add a pound of lard that you will chop; when your farce is well chopped, spread it to a thickness of one inch on your shoulder
5. Lay lardons and pieces of scarlet tongue, truffes if you have them; some people add an omelette made with spinach green, and if you wish, carrots also cut into lardons
6. To marble your galantine, cover all these ingredients with farce, and add more lardons, truffes, etc., then another layer of farce
7. When you have used it all, roll your shoulder lengthwise; join your flesh well together; tie it and cover it with lard
8. Wrap it tightly in canvas or a clean white cloth; tie it once more so that as it cooks it maintains a beautiful form
9. Place it in a braising pan where you have laid some slices of lard, some slices of veal, two blanched veal feet, the bones from your shoulder, six carrots, eight or ten onions, one of which is studded with four cloves, four bay leaves, a little thyme, a large bouquet of parsley and chives
10. Moisten with beef broth; if you do not have it, use water and salt
11. Bring your cooking liquid to boil and braise for three hours; test with a skewer to determine doneness

title: Calf's Tails in Terrine (Queues de veau en terrine)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Seven or eight calf's tails
- Butter
- Flour, four spoonfuls
- Beef consommé, four spoonfuls
- Mushrooms, turned
- Parsley and chives
- Veal quenelles
- Small pieces of veal shoulder
- Veal sweetbreads, in pieces
- Butter for sautéing the sweetbreads
- Velouté, one spoonful
- Egg yolks, five

## Directions

1. Cut your tails at the joints; only the section from the thick end to the middle of the tail yields four or five pieces
2. Sauté them in a good piece of butter without letting them brown, as this ragout must be white
3. When your meat is well browned in the butter, add four spoonfuls of flour and blend it with your butter and tail
4. Then add four spoonfuls of consommé
5. Stir your ragout until it boils; take care to skim it well
6. Add a portion of turned mushrooms, a bouquet of parsley and chives
7. Cook your ragout and degrease it when it is three-quarters cooked
8. When your ragout is completely cooked, remove the pieces of tail with a slotted spoon, along with your mushrooms, and place them in another saucepan
9. Pour one spoonful of velouté into the sauce of your tails
10. Reduce this sauce; when you see it is sufficiently reduced and well thickened, strain it through a sieve
11. Poach veal quenelles in your ragout; drain them at the moment of serving and place them in your terrine
12. Mix in small pieces of veal shoulder and pieces of veal sweetbreads that you have sautéed in butter; drain them when cooked and place them in your terrine
13. Add to your tail ragout a liaison of five egg yolks
14. Stir it gently on the stove until your ragout is thickened; be careful it does not boil for fear it will curdle
15. Pour it into your terrine over your garnishes, which you will have kept warm; the sauce of your ragout must be abundant so that all the garnishes are bathed in it

title: Calf's Tails in White Sauce (Queues de Veau au blanc)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's tails
- Butter
- Flour
- Water
- Parsley and chives
- Bay leaves
- Mushrooms
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- Small onions
- Egg yolks, four or more depending on the strength of the ragout

## Directions

1. Prepare your tails as the previous recipe; sauté them in butter and add flour in the same manner
2. Moisten with water
3. Add a bouquet of parsley and chives, two bay leaves, mushrooms, salt and a little coarse pepper
4. Skim it; when it is three-quarters cooked, degrease it
5. Add small, well-trimmed onions of uniform size; do not let them cook too much lest they become mushy
6. At the moment of serving, blend in a liaison of four egg yolks, more or less according to how strong your ragout is
7. Ensure it is well seasoned with salt. This ragout can be made brown or white; you would make a roux

title: Canard au Père Douillet (Duck Father Douillet)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- bouquet of parsley
- chives
- one clove of garlic
- two cloves
- thyme
- bay leaf
- basil
- coriander
- onion slices
- one carrot
- one parsnip
- butter
- two glasses of bouillon
- one glass of white wine

## Directions

1. Singe and clean the duck well; empty it and truss the legs in a pan just to its size.
2. Add a bouquet of parsley, chives, one clove of garlic, two cloves, thyme, bay leaf, basil, a good pinch of coriander, onion slices, one carrot, one parsnip, a piece of butter, two glasses of bouillon, and one glass of white wine.
3. Cook over low heat; when the duck yields under your finger, pass the sauce through a sieve and degrease it.
4. Reduce the sauce over the fire to the consistency of a sauce.
5. Serve under the duck.
6. You can also serve it the same way by cutting it into four pieces before cooking.

title: Canard aux Navets (Duck with Turnips)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- butter
- flour
- bouillon
- bouquet garni
- salt
- pepper
- turnips

## Directions

1. Singe the duck; clean, empty, and truss the legs inside.
2. Put a little butter in a pan with a spoonful of flour; brown it to a nice color and moisten with bouillon.
3. Put the duck in with a bouquet garni, a little salt, and coarse pepper.
4. Have turnips properly cut, which you cook with the duck: if they are hard, cook everything at the same time; if they are not, add them halfway through the duck's cooking.
5. When your ragout is well cooked and degreased, add a little coulis.
6. Serve short sauce. This is the way to make duck with turnips in the homestyle fashion.
7. The other way is to cook the duck separately in a white braise, and prepare the turnips turned into ovals, blanched and cooked with bouillon, veal jus and coulis; when your ragout is made, serve it over the duck.

title: Canard aux Navets (Duck with Turnips)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- duck
- turnips
- butter
- sugar
- salt
- pepper

## Directions

1. Truss and cook your duck as you would cook duck in a pan
2. Turn your turnips, that is to say cut them all to the same size
3. Sauté them in butter; when they are well browned, let them drain
4. Put them with your duck; cook on a low fire
5. Degrease it; add a small piece of sugar
6. At the moment of serving, remove the trussing string, and pour your ragout of turnips over it
7. Check that it is properly seasoned with salt

title: Canard aux Navets (Duck with Turnips)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- five or six turnips

## Directions

1. Cook your duck as the previous one
2. Put five or six turnips cut into slices in the cooking
3. At service time, drain, unbind and dress it
4. Put your ragout of small turnips under or over your duck

title: Canard aux Olives (Duck with Olives)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- duck
- olives
- Spanish sauce (espagnole)
- consommé or bouillon
- coarse pepper
- light roux
- silk sieve

## Directions

1. Truss and cook your duck as you would cook duck in a pan
2. Turn your olives, that is to say remove the flesh from the pit in a corkscrew manner
3. Keep them whole so they resume their original form
4. If you wish, let them boil in water, or put them in a saucepan
5. Put in four spoonfuls of Spanish sauce (espagnole), twice as much consommé or bouillon, a little coarse pepper
6. Cook your olives on a hot fire; when the sauce is reduced to one third, remove them from the fire
7. Keep them warm in a bain-marie
8. If you have no sauce, make a light roux, which you will moisten with the cooking liquid from your duck
9. Pass it through a silk sieve
10. If your sauce is not thick enough, add bouillon
11. At the moment of serving, drain your duck, remove the trussing string, arrange it on your plate, and put your ragout of olives underneath

title: Canard aux Olives (Duck with Olives)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- olives

## Directions

1. Truss and cook your duck as the braised duck
2. Turn your olives, that is to say, remove the meat from the pit in a corkscrew motion, keeping it whole so it resumes its original form

title: Canard en Aiguillettes (Duck in Strips)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- duck
- shallots
- veal stock (blond de veau)
- coarse pepper
- nutmeg
- salt
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Truss, tie, and cook your duck as you would cook duck in a pan
2. When it is three-quarters cooked, at the moment of serving, drain and remove the trussing string
3. Serve it on your plate; make eight cuts, four on each side on the breast
4. Chop shallots very finely, one spoonful, which you put in a saucepan with veal stock two spoonfuls, coarse pepper, a little grated nutmeg, a little salt
5. Let your shallots boil a few times; remove from the fire
6. Press the juice of two lemons into your sauce; pour it over the incisions in your duck
7. You can serve your duck as an entrée on the spit, or simply on the spit

title: Canard en Chausson (Duck en Chausson)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- farce de volaille or godiveau
- white wine
- bouillon
- bouquet garni
- salt
- pepper
- coulis

## Directions

1. Bone and stuff the duck as for the stuffed duck (page 164).
2. Cook it with one glass of white wine and as much bouillon, a bouquet garni, salt, and coarse pepper.
3. When cooked, pass the sauce through a sieve, degrease it, and add a little coulis to bind it.
4. Reduce to the consistency of a sauce.
5. Serve over the duck.

title: Canard en Chausson (Duck in Pastry)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One duck
- One glass of white wine
- Equal amount of bouillon
- Bouquet garni
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- Coulis

## Directions

1. Debone and stuff the duck as for Canard Farci (page 164)
2. Cook it with a glass of white wine and an equal amount of bouillon, a bouquet garni, salt, and coarse pepper
3. When cooked, pass the sauce through a sieve
4. Degrease it and add a little coulis to bind it
5. Reduce to sauce consistency
6. Serve over the duck

title: Canard en Hochepot (Duck Stew)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- turnips
- quarter of a cabbage
- parsnips
- carrots
- onions
- good bouillon
- piece of small lard with rind
- bouquet garni
- salt
- coulis

## Directions

1. Singe and clean the duck, and cut it into four pieces.
2. Cook properly cut and turned turnips, quarter of a cabbage, parsnips, carrots, and onions in a small pot.
3. Blanch everything for half a quarter hour.
4. Place them in the small pot with good bouillon, a piece of small lard cut in slices with rind attached and tied, a bouquet garni, and little salt.
5. When everything is cooked, arrange the duck in a serving terrine; place all the vegetables around it.
6. Degrease the bouillon from the small pot where your vegetables cooked; add a little coulis, and serve short sauce over the vegetables and duck.
7. Taste your sauce beforehand to ensure it is of good flavor.

title: Canard Farci (Stuffed Duck)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- poultry forcemeat or godiveau forcemeat
- veal round the size of an egg
- beef fat twice the amount of veal
- parsley
- chives
- mushrooms
- two raw eggs
- salt
- pepper
- half a setier of cream

## Directions

1. Singe and clean the duck through the vent, and bone it entirely without piercing the skin.
2. Begin deboning from the vent, turning it inside out as you remove the bones.
3. Fill it halfway with poultry forcemeat or godiveau.
4. To make godiveau forcemeat: take veal round the size of an egg and twice as much beef fat, chop them together; add parsley, chives, and mushrooms all chopped, two raw eggs, salt, pepper, and half a setier of cream; mix well together and place in the body of the duck.
5. Tie it up so nothing escapes.
6. Cook it in a braise like beef tongue (page 29).
7. When cooked, wipe off the fat and serve with a good sauce or a ragout of chestnuts.
8. For the chestnut ragout: cook chestnuts with half a setier of white wine, a little coulis, and a pinch of salt, and serve as you see fit.

title: Canard Poêlé Sauce Bigarade (Braised Duck with Bitter Orange Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck, plucked, cleaned and singed
- string for trussing
- lard for barding
- lemon juice
- espagnole sauce, worked
- coarse pepper
- bigarade juice
- bigarade zest

## Directions

1. Truss the duck's legs inside the thighs and tie with string
2. Tuck the rump into the body to shorten it and secure with trussing needle and string
3. Give your duck a round and shortened shape
4. Rub the breast with lemon juice
5. Place lard rinds in your casserole and put the duck on top
6. Cover it with lard rinds and place a cover for cooking
7. One hour before serving, put it on the fire and braise until service time
8. Drain, unbind, and dress it on the dish
9. Put three spoonfuls of worked espagnole, a little coarse pepper, the juice of a bigarade with a little of its zest in a sauce
10. Put this sauce on the fire; at the first boil pour it under your duck. If you lack bigarade, use lemon.

title: Canard à l'Italienne (Duck Italian Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One duck
- Half glass of white wine
- Equal amount of bouillon
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- Two spoonfuls of oil
- Parsley
- Chives
- Mushrooms
- One clove of garlic
- Flour

## Directions

1. Cook a duck with half glass of white wine and an equal amount of bouillon, salt, and coarse pepper
2. In a casserole, place two spoonfuls of oil with chopped parsley, chives, mushrooms, and one clove of garlic
3. Pass them over the fire
4. Add a pinch of flour
5. Moisten with the duck's cooking liquid, which should be degreased and passed through a sieve
6. Reduce to sauce consistency
7. Degrease and serve over the duck

title: Canard à la Bruxelles (Duck Brussels Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck
- veal sweetbread cut in dice
- small lard well interlarded
- parsley
- chives
- mushrooms
- two shallots
- salt
- pepper
- lard bard
- one glass of white wine
- bouillon
- two onions
- one carrot
- half a parsnip
- bouquet garni
- coulis

## Directions

1. Singe and empty the duck.
2. Make a salpicon as follows: cut veal sweetbread in dice with small lard well interlarded; mix everything with parsley, chives, mushrooms, two shallots all chopped, little salt, and coarse pepper.
3. Place this in the body of the duck; sew it up so nothing escapes.
4. Cook it with a bard of lard on the breast, one glass of white wine, as much bouillon, two onions, one carrot, half a parsnip, and a bouquet garni.
5. When cooked, pass the sauce through a sieve, degrease it, add a little coulis to bind it; reduce to the consistency of a sauce.
6. Serve over the duck.

title: Canard à la Béarnoise (Duck Béarnaise Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One duck
- Bouillon
- Half glass of white wine
- Bouquet of parsley, chives, thyme, laurel, basil
- Two cloves
- Seven or eight large onions
- Butter
- Flour
- Vinegar

## Directions

1. Cook the duck with a little bouillon and half glass of white wine, and a bouquet of parsley, chives, thyme, laurel, basil, and two cloves
2. In a casserole, place seven or eight large onions cut into slices with a piece of butter
3. Pass them over the fire, turning often until they are colored
4. Add a good pinch of flour
5. Moisten with the duck's cooking liquid
6. Cook the onions and reduce to short sauce
7. Degrease it and add a dash of vinegar
8. Serve over the duck

title: Canard à la Purée de Lentilles (Duck with Lentil Puree)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one duck, prepared as for braised duck
- lard for barding
- slices of veal shank
- two carrots
- three onions
- two cloves
- one bay leaf
- thyme
- a bouquet of parsley and chives
- one spoonful of bouillon

## Directions

1. Prepare your duck as the braised duck
2. Place lard rinds in the bottom of a casserole and put the duck on top
3. Add a few slices of veal shank, two carrots, three onions, two cloves, a bay leaf, a little thyme, a bouquet of parsley and chives
4. Cover your duck with lard rinds and add one spoonful of bouillon
5. If your duck is tender, three quarters of an hour is sufficient to cook it; if it is tough, leave it longer on the fire
6. At service time, drain, unbind and dress it on your dish
7. Mask it, if you wish, with a lentil puree. If your duck is very white and well fleshed, put the puree underneath
8. You can also serve it with turnip or pea puree

title: Canard à la Purée Verte (Duck with Green Pea Purée)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One duck
- Half-litre of dried peas or one litre of fresh peas
- Bouillon
- Parsley
- Chives
- Salt
- Pepper
- Bouquet of parsley, chives, thyme, laurel, basil
- Half clove of garlic
- Two cloves
- Optional: small piece of bacon with rind

## Directions

1. Cook half-litre of dried peas with a little bouillon, parsley, and chive tops; if using fresh peas, use one litre and omit the parsley and chives
2. Pass the peas through a sieve to make a thick purée
3. Cook a duck with bouillon, salt, pepper, a bouquet of parsley, chives, thyme, laurel, basil, half a clove of garlic, and two cloves
4. When the duck is cooked, pass its sauce through a sieve and add it to the purée to give it body
5. Reduce the purée until it is neither too thin nor too thick
6. Optionally, cook a piece of small bacon cut in slices with the rind attached and serve it around the duck
7. Serve the duck

title: Caneton au Pois (Duckling with Peas)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One or two ducklings
- Flour
- Butter
- Bouillon
- One litre of small peas
- Bouquet of parsley and chives
- Salt

## Directions

1. Scald and eviscerate one or two ducklings
2. Truss the legs so that only the claws show
3. Blanch them briefly in boiling water
4. Make a light roux with two pinches of flour and a piece of butter
5. Moisten with bouillon
6. Add the ducklings with one litre of small peas and a bouquet of parsley and chives
7. Boil gently until the ducklings are cooked
8. A moment before serving, add a little salt
9. Serve with short sauce

title: Carbonnade Panée à l'Anglaise (Breaded Braised Meat English Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- carbonnades
- salt
- pepper
- warm butter
- breadcrumbs
- eggs
- butter for basting

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your carbonnades as the previous ones
2. When they are cooked, drain them
3. Dust them with fine salt and pepper
4. Dip them in warm butter
5. Put them in breadcrumbs
6. When they are breaded, let them cool
7. Beat four eggs that you mix in the remaining butter
8. Dip your carbonnades in it, and then put them in breadcrumbs
9. Make sure there is breadcrumb coverage everywhere
10. Put them on a saucepan lid
11. Brush them with warm butter using a feather brush
12. Sprinkle breadcrumbs on them
13. Blow on the top so there is not too much
14. Half an hour before serving, put them on the grill on a gentle fire

title: Carbonnades aux Concombres (Braised Meat with Cucumbers)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- saddle of meat cut into pieces
- lard strips
- veal slices
- carrots
- onions
- bay leaf
- thyme
- buttered paper
- bouillon
- cucumbers à la crème
- salt
- pepper

## Directions

1. From the saddle, cut pieces four fingers long
2. Remove the bones found under the fillet
3. Season the underside with salt and coarse pepper
4. Tie it so it has a square-long shape
5. Put lard strips in the bottom of your saucepan with a few veal slices
6. Place your carbonnades on top
7. Add two carrots, two onions, a bay leaf, a little thyme
8. Cover them with lard strips and a round of buttered paper
9. Fill a small spoon with bouillon
10. Simmer them for two hours
11. At serving time, drain them, remove the first skin from the top, glaze them, and put cucumbers à la crème on the platter with your carbonnades on top

title: Carbonnades à la Jardinière (Braised Meat with Mixed Vegetables)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- carbonnades
- small roots turned into batons
- glazed lettuce
- light roux
- sugar

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your carbonnades as the previous ones
2. At serving time, drain them, remove the skin covering the fat, glaze them, and arrange them around the platter: one glazed lettuce, one carbonnade, and so on
3. Put small roots turned into batons in the middle
4. If you do not have sauce for your small roots, make a light roux that you will moisten with the liquid in which your carbonnades cooked, and put your roots in it with a small piece of sugar

title: Carbonnades à la Purée de Champignons (Braised Meat with Mushroom Purée)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- carbonnades
- mushroom purée

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your carbonnades as those with cucumbers
2. At serving time, drain them, remove the first skin from the top, glaze them, and put them on a mushroom purée

title: Cardes Poirées (Chard with Leeks)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- chard
- water
- flour
- butter
- salt
- pepper
- vinegar

## Directions

1. Peel and wash the chard
2. Cook them in water, stirring from time to time so the top does not blacken
3. When cooked, place them to drain
4. Make a white sauce with a pinch of flour, water, butter, salt, pepper, and a dash of vinegar
5. Make it thicken over the fire and put the chard to boil a little moment over low heat so they take flavor
6. If the butter turned to oil, it would be a sign that the sauce is too thick; put in a spoonful of water and stir until the sauce returns as before

title: Cardes-Poirées au fromage (Swiss Chard with Cheese)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Swiss chard whites
- Water
- Salt
- Butter
- White sauce
- Gruyère cheese
- Parmesan cheese
- Breadcrumbs

## Directions

1. Cut the white parts of Swiss chard to a length of four inches
2. Fill a saucepan with water and add salt and butter the size of an egg
3. When water boils, add the Swiss chard
4. Boil for three-quarters of an hour until the chard bends under your fingers
5. Drain well
6. Make a slightly bound white sauce with grated Gruyère and Parmesan cheese mixed in
7. Mix the drained chard into the sauce and sauté
8. Butter a dish and arrange in alternating layers of chard and grated cheese until full
9. Cover with grated cheese
10. Dip feathers in warm butter and drizzle over the cheese
11. Cover the chard with breadcrumbs
12. When well breaded, drizzle a little more butter over
13. Half an hour before serving, place the dish on red coals with a hot camp oven on top until it takes on a nice color
14. Blot the butter from the edges with soft breadcrumbs

title: Cardons (Cardoons)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- tender and white cardoon ribs
- water
- salt
- blanc

## Directions

1. Take the most tender and whitest ribs of the cardoons
2. They must be full and firm inside when cut
3. All ribs should be the same length
4. Cut the edges and blanch in plenty of water
5. Test from time to time to ensure the slime is coming off
6. When it comes off easily, remove from the boiling water and add cold water so you can use your hands
7. Detach the slime and place the cardoons in fresh water
8. Trim again and drain
9. Prepare a blanc and cook them three-quarters of the way

title: Cardons au Consommé (Cardoons in Consommé)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- cardoons
- blanc
- consommé

## Directions

1. Cook the cardoons in blanc
2. Drain them, trim, and pare them
3. Ensure they are all the same size and length
4. Place them in a casserole and pour consommé over just enough to cover them
5. Boil on high heat
6. Reduce the liquid to three-quarters
7. Arrange the cardoons on the plate and use the reduced consommé as sauce

title: Cardons au Velouté (Cardoons in Velouté Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- cardoons
- blanc
- coarse pepper
- velouté
- consommé
- butter
- flour
- bouillon
- bay leaf
- lemon juice

## Directions

1. Cook the cardoons in blanc as described in the first article
2. Drain and trim them, ensuring they are all the same size
3. Place them in a casserole with a little coarse pepper, 5 spoonfuls of velouté, and twice as much consommé
4. Cook on high heat
5. When the sauce is reduced to more than half, arrange the cardoons on the plate and pour the sauce over
6. Alternatively, simply blanch the cardoons, remove the slime, trim, and place in a casserole
7. In another vessel, make a white roux with a quarter pound of butter and a spoonful of flour
8. Moisten with bouillon, keeping the sauce thin
9. Pour it over the cardoons
10. Add a little coarse pepper, a bay leaf, and the juice of a lemon
11. Cook them in their sauce

title: Cardons d'Espagne (Spanish Cardoons)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- cardoons
- water
- bouillon
- flour
- salt
- onions
- roots
- bouquet of fine herbs
- verjus or verjus in grain
- butter
- coulis

## Directions

1. Cut them to the length of three inches, do not use those that are hollow and green
2. Cook them half an hour in water and remove them in lukewarm water to peel them
3. Cook them with bouillon in which you have dissolved a spoonful of flour, add salt, onions, roots, a bouquet of fine herbs, a dash of verjus or verjus in grain, a little butter
4. When cooked, remove them and put them in a good coulis with a little bouillon
5. Make them boil half an hour in this sauce so they take flavor and serve them
6. The sauce should be neither too clear nor too thick, and of a beautiful blonde color
7. If you wish to serve them in the lean way, put them in a sauce as is said for chard with leeks

title: Cardons à l'Espagnole (Cardoons Spanish Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- cardoons
- five spoonfuls of Spanish sauce
- twice as much consommé
- roux (optional)

## Directions

1. Prepare the cardoons as previously described
2. Add five spoonfuls of Spanish sauce and twice as much consommé
3. Reduce by more than half
4. Arrange the cardoons on the plate
5. If the sauce is not reduced enough, place it on high heat and pour it under the cardoons
6. If using only roux, moisten it as described previously and add a little juice

title: Cardons à la Béchamelle (Cardoons with Béchamel Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- cardoons
- consommé
- four spoonfuls of béchamel sauce
- two egg yolks
- butter, the size of half an egg

## Directions

1. Prepare the cardoons as previously described
2. Let them finish cooking in consommé
3. Transfer them to another pot
4. Reduce the consommé in which the cardoons cooked almost to a glaze
5. Add four spoonfuls of béchamel sauce
6. Let it come to a boil a few times
7. Sauce the cardoons with this mixture
8. When the sauce is reduced, bind it with two egg yolks and butter the size of half an egg, stirring into the sauce
9. Pass the sauce through a sieve over the cardoons

title: Carlets grillés (Grilled Plaice)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- plaice
- oil
- salt
- pepper
- straw tubes
- lean Italian sauce

## Directions

1. Clean and wash the plaice
2. Dry them
3. Oil them and add salt and pepper
4. Place straw tubes on the grill and plaice over them
5. Grill over a small fire
6. Coat with a lean Italian sauce

title: Carlets à la bonne eau (Plaice in Good Water)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- plaice
- good water

## Directions

1. Clean and prepare the plaice
2. Cook them in good water
3. Serve them with the cooking water

title: Carottes au Beurre (Carrots with Butter)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 10 or 12 carrots of the same size
- water
- salt
- butter, the size of a walnut
- coarse pepper
- grated nutmeg
- velouté or water
- fine herbs

## Directions

1. Peel 10 or 12 carrots of the same size
2. Cut them into rounds, one and a half lines thick
3. Blanch them in water with salt and a walnut-sized piece of butter
4. When they are cooked but still firm, drain them in a colander
5. Put them in a casserole with a good piece of butter, salt, coarse pepper, and a little grated nutmeg
6. Sauté them on the fire
7. Pour a spoonful of velouté or water only so the butter does not turn to oil
8. Do not let them boil; they should only be very hot
9. You can add fine herbs

title: Carpe au Bleu (Carp au Bleu)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- large carp
- vinegar
- court-bouillon

## Directions

1. Gut the carp and remove the gills, do not scale it
2. Place it on a large platter
3. Boil vinegar and pour it boiling over the carp, which will make it turn blue
4. Cook it also in court-bouillon
5. When it is cooked, serve it on a napkin garnished with fresh parsley as a lean main course dish

title: Carpe au Bleu (Carp in Blue)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Carp
- Red wine
- Onions
- Carrots
- Parsley
- Six bay leaves
- Thyme
- Three cloves
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Clean your carp and make a very small opening; be very careful to remove the slime from your carp
2. Tie the head
3. Put it in your fish kettle
4. Boil a pint of red wine and pour it boiling hot over your carp; let it be completely bathed in the red wine
5. Take seven or eight onions cut into slices, four carrots, a handful of parsley, six bay leaves, a branch of thyme, three cloves, salt and pepper
6. Simmer it for an hour, or longer if your carp is large; then let it cool
7. Arrange a napkin on a platter, put your carp on top, and parsley around it

title: Carpe au Court-Bouillon (Carp in Court-Bouillon)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Carp
- Vinegar
- Court-bouillon

## Directions

1. Prepare your carp as the previous one; instead of hot wine boil vinegar and pour it over; make a court-bouillon which you put with your carp

title: Carpe en Matelotte (Carp in Matelote Sauce)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- carp
- pike
- eel
- crayfish
- barbel
- river fish
- butter
- flour
- small onions
- red wine
- lean broth
- salt
- pepper
- bouquet garni
- fine herbs
- bread croutons

## Directions

1. Scale and remove the gills from the carp, then cut it into sections and place in a casserole with other fish such as pike, eel, crayfish, barbel, or other river fish as available.
2. In another casserole, make a small roux with butter and a spoonful of flour.
