2. Mix everything together and knead until your dough is well combined and you can flatten it with a rolling pin
3. When your dough is very thin, cut it into thin strips, taking care to dust them with flour so they do not stick together
4. Have good bouillon and drop your shredded dough into it when it is boiling
5. Half an hour is sufficient for your dough to be sufficiently cooked
6. Degrease your soup well; add a little coarse pepper just before putting it in the tureen
7. You can also make this dough with egg whites alone; add a little nutmeg and coarse pepper according to taste

title: Soup with Semolina (Semolina Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- bouillon, passed through silk sieve
- semolina
- veal stock or coloring as needed

## Directions

1. Pass bouillon through a silk sieve into a saucepan
2. When it boils, take semolina and pour it into your boiling bouillon
3. Stir carefully with a large spoon so that the semolina does not stick or form lumps
4. After half an hour, remove it from the fire; it will be sufficiently cooked
5. Be careful to degrease your soup; if it is too pale, color it with veal stock or whatever you have to give it a beautiful color
6. Ensure it has good flavor and proper seasoning

title: Soup with Vermicelle (Vermicelli Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- good bouillon, passed through silk sieve
- vermicelli from Italy

## Directions

1. Ensure your vermicelli does not smell old and has no off taste; Italian vermicelli is the best
2. Have good bouillon that you have passed through a silk sieve, sufficient for your soup
3. Bring it to a boil; when it boils, add your vermicelli so that it does not clump together
4. When it has boiled for a good half hour, remove it from the fire so it does not become too mushy and your soup is clear
5. A half pound suffices for eight or ten persons; it should not be thick

title: Soup à la Deselignac (Deselignac Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- fifteen egg yolks
- one pint of good bouillon

## Directions

1. Dilute fifteen egg yolks with one pint of good bouillon, passing it several times through cheesecloth
2. Put your mixture in a mold or vessel so you can set it in a bain-marie
3. When it is well set, pour hot bouillon into your tureen
4. With a skimmer, take some of your eggs (prepared in the bain-marie) in such a way that it forms portions that you put in your tureen; your soup should be well garnished with it

title: Soup à la Geaufret (Geaufret Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- ten or twelve red potatoes
- four chicken breasts
- six to eight raw egg yolks
- nutmeg
- coarse pepper
- optional: double cream
- bouillon or salted water with a little butter

## Directions

1. Cook ten or twelve red potatoes in hot ashes
2. When they are cooked, peel them; remove all the roasted part and anything hard, taking only the mealy part which you pound dry
3. Add four chicken breasts that you pound well together
4. When everything is well mixed and without lumps, add six or eight raw egg yolks, which you pound with your potatoes and fillets several times, along with a little nutmeg and coarse pepper
5. When everything is well combined, if your dough is too thick, add a little double cream so that you can place your dough on a spoon (like quenelles) or roll it like small balls
6. Poach them in bouillon or in salted water to which you add a little butter
7. After they have been poached (that is, cooked half an hour in boiling water), drain them
8. Put good bouillon in your tureen and add your quenelles
9. Ensure everything has proper seasoning

title: Soup à la Polacre (Polacre Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- potatoes
- good bouillon

## Directions

1. When your potatoes are cooked in water, cut them into slices like coins
2. Bring good bouillon to a boil in which you

title: Soup à la Polacre (Polish-Style Potato Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- potatoes, cooked
- good broth
- a handful of fennel

## Directions

1. Cut the cooked potatoes into slices like coins
2. Bring good broth to a boil in which you put a handful of bundled fennel and let everything boil for a quarter hour
3. Pour your broth over the potatoes that you will have put in your soup tureen
4. Be sure there are enough potatoes to take the place of bread

title: Soup à la Quenelle (Quenelle Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- flour
- egg yolks
- eggs
- salt
- bouillon
- nutmeg
- good bouillon

## Directions

1. Make the same batter as for Xavier soup, except without parsley
2. Make it thick enough so that when you put three quarters of a soup spoon of it, you can make the dough flow with your finger, and when it falls into your boiling bouillon, it forms a round, long or oval ball
3. Let your soup cook for half an hour
4. Take care to degrease it and ensure it has proper seasoning
5. It can be made without meat

title: Soup à la Xavier (Xavier Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- three quarters of a liter of flour
- six egg yolks
- two whole eggs
- a little salt
- bouillon (sufficient for the batter to be liquid enough to pass through a perforated spoon)
- one spoonful of finely chopped parsley
- one quarter nutmeg, grated
- a pinch of coarse pepper
- good bouillon

## Directions

1. Dilute three quarters of a liter of flour with six egg yolks and two whole eggs, a little salt and bouillon (sufficiently so that your mixture is liquid enough to pass through a perforated spoon)
2. Add one spoonful of very finely chopped parsley that you mix with your dough, then one quarter grated nutmeg, a pinch of coarse pepper
3. When everything is well mixed, add to three quarters of a saucepan of good bouillon
4. When it boils, take a perforated spoon into which you pour your mixture and let it fall into your bouillon
5. Ensure it boils constantly so your dough sets
6. Take care to skim your soup so it is clear
7. This soup does not need to boil more than a quarter hour
8. You can also make this soup without meat using lean bouillon

title: Soupe à l'Oignon (Onion Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- onions
- a quarter pound of butter
- water
- salt and fine pepper
- bread

## Directions

1. Peel your onions, cut them in half
2. Cut off the head and tail to avoid the acrid taste of the onion
3. Before putting your onions cut into slices in your casserole, melt a quarter pound of butter in it
4. Fry or brown your onion until it is well blond
5. Put in enough water for your soup, seasoned with salt and fine pepper, and let it boil for a quarter hour
6. Pour your broth over the bread and serve your soup

title: Stewed Partridges (Perdrix Étouffées)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- three old partridges
- medium lardons (bacon strips)
- salt
- coarse pepper
- aromatic herbs
- barding lard
- veal slices
- carrots
- onions
- cloves
- parsley and chives
- bay leaf
- thyme
- buttered paper
- white wine
- bouillon
- espagnole sauce
- game stock

## Directions

1. Pluck and clean three old partridges
2. Singe lightly
3. Lard with medium lardons seasoned with salt, coarse pepper, and pounded aromatics
4. Truss the legs as for a braised hen and tie securely
5. Place bards of lard in a casserole and arrange partridges on top
6. Add a few slices of veal, two carrots, two onions, two cloves, a bouquet of parsley and chives, a bay leaf, and a little thyme
7. Cover with bards of lard and place a round of buttered paper on top
8. Add a glass of white wine and a glass of bouillon with a little salt
9. Simmer the partridges for an hour and a half, depending on how tough they are
10. At service, drain and untie them
11. Add three tablespoons of espagnole and three tablespoons of game stock
12. Reduce the sauce by half and sauce the partridges
13. If you do not have espagnole, make a light roux and moisten with the cooking liquid from the partridges
14. Pass through a silk sieve, reduce the sauce by half so it develops flavor, degrease and strain through cheesecloth
15. Pour under the partridges

title: Stuffed Head Lettuce (Laitues Pommées et Farcies)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- 8 to 12 head lettuces, depending on size
- meat forcemeat seasoned to taste
- flour
- white wine
- oil
- salt
- egg, beaten
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Boil the lettuces for half an hour in water, then remove them to fresh water and press them well with your hands
2. Spread out on a table the leaves of each lettuce without separating them
3. Put in the middle a forcemeat of meat seasoned to taste, as you will find below for small pâtés
4. Wrap the forcemeat with the lettuce leaves, tie them and cook them in a small braising pan
5. When they are cooked, drain and press them in a cloth
6. Dip them in a batter made with flour, white wine and a spoonful of oil, a little salt, and fry them to a nice color
7. You can also dip them in beaten egg, coat them with bread crumbs; being stuffed in this manner and cooked in the braising pan, they serve to garnish meat entrées

title: Stuffed Sheep's Feet (Pieds de Mouton farcis)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Sheep's feet, deboned
- Poultry quenelle farce with finely chopped herbs, salt, coarse pepper, and grated nutmeg
- Or godiveau with the same seasoning
- Half a pound of grated lard
- Half a pound of beef fat
- Half a pound of veal round cut into dice
- Butter, the size of two eggs
- Two lemons cut into slices without the white
- Two carrots cut into dice
- Two onions
- Two cloves
- Two bay leaves
- Thyme
- Salt
- Water
- Velouté, reduced
- Two eggs
- Coarse pepper
- Grated nutmeg
- Lemon juice

## Directions

1. After deboning your sheep's feet, fill them with poultry quenelle farce in which you put finely chopped herbs, a little salt (because in cooking it will lose its seasoning), a little coarse pepper, a little grated nutmeg
2. If you have no quenelle farce, use godiveau with the seasoning described
3. Sew the end closed with a needle and thread so the farce does not come out
4. Blanch them, refresh them, drain them, dry them
5. Flame them and remove the hair found in the cleft of the foot
6. Put in a casserole half a pound of grated lard, half a pound of beef fat, half a pound of veal round cut into dice, butter the size of two eggs, two lemons cut into slices without the white, two carrots cut into dice, two onions, two cloves, two bay leaves, and thyme
7. Pass everything together over the fire
8. When it has browned a bit, add salt and one spoonful of water
9. When your blanc boils, arrange your twelve or fifteen sheep's feet in a casserole and pour your blanc over them
10. Put a double round of buttered paper so your feet do not blacken
11. Let them simmer on very low heat for four hours
12. After this time, test them to see if they are cooked
13. At the moment of service, drain them, cut the two extremities, remove the thread, arrange them on the plate
14. Coat them with a velouté that has been reduced, in which you put a liaison of two eggs, a little coarse pepper, a little grated nutmeg, and the juice of a lemon
15. Check that your sauce is properly salted
16. These feet can be put with different sauces, such as tomato sauce, Spanish sauce, onion purée, or Provençale style

title: Surmulet et Maquereau Grillé (Grilled Red Mullet and Mackerel)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- red mullet or mackerel
- salt
- pepper
- oil
- sauce with capers and anchovies

## Directions

1. For red mullet: scale, clean, wash well, and cut slightly on both sides
2. For mackerel: only clean, wash well, and slit along the back
3. Dry both well in a cloth
4. Soak them for half an hour with salt, pepper, and oil
5. Grill them, basting with the marinade while cooking
6. When cooked, serve with a white sauce with capers and anchovies

title: Tablettes de Réglisse pour le Rhume (Licorice Tablets for Cough)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One pint of river water
- One pound of scraped green licorice cut into very small pieces
- Two handfuls of barley
- Four rainette apples
- One pound of clarified sugar
- Two ounces of gum tragacanth

## Directions

1. Put the water, licorice, barley, and apples in an earthenware pot and bring to boil over very low heat for four to five hours, until well cooked and reduced to less than a chopine of water.
2. Crush everything together and force through a sieve with all your strength.
3. Add the clarified sugar and the melted gum tragacanth to what you have strained.
4. Dry this composition over the fire, stirring constantly with a wooden spoon until it no longer sticks to your fingers.
5. Pour it onto slate tablets or copper sheets rubbed with a little oil.
6. When cold, cut into tablets and put them to dry in a moderately warm place.

title: Talmouses (Cheese Pastries)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- a piece of water
- half a quarter pound of butter
- salt
- two tablespoons of flour
- eggs
- fresh cheese with cream, well drained and made the same day
- puff pastry dough

## Directions

1. Put water, butter, and a little salt in a saucepan; bring to boil
2. Add two tablespoons of flour that you thin well until your dough is firm
3. Remove from fire and thin in as many eggs as the dough can absorb without becoming liquid
4. Add fresh white cheese with cream that is well drained and made the same day, mixing it with your dough
5. Take molds for small pastries
6. Put in an abaisse of puff pastry dough, rolled out very thin
7. Place on small pastry molds so it overhangs at four corners
8. Place your cheese dough about the size of a small egg on top and wrap it with the four corners of the puff pastry
9. Gild with beaten egg
10. Bake in the oven with gentle heat
11. When cooked and golden colored, serve hot as an entremet

title: Tanche à la Poulette (Tench Poulette Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- tench
- butter
- flour
- white wine
- salt
- pepper
- bay leaf
- parsley
- chives
- small onions
- mushrooms
- egg yolks

## Directions

1. Put your tench for a minute in a pot of almost boiling water; remove it with your knife
2. Remove its slime and scales; cut it into pieces and let it disgorge
3. Put butter in a saucepan; warm it with your pieces of tench
4. Sauté them in the butter; add a spoonful of flour that you mix together
5. Moisten your stew with a bottle of white wine, salt, coarse pepper, a bay leaf, a bouquet of parsley and chives, small onions, and mushrooms
6. Cook your stew fairly quickly; when it is cooked, add a liaison of three egg yolks
7. Remove the bay leaf and the bouquet; check that it tastes good

title: Tartelettes (Small Tarts)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- pâte feuilletée (puff pastry)
- crème de frangipane or jam
- egg for glazing
- sugar and cochineal for glazing

## Directions

1. Make a puff pastry as indicated previously
2. Roll it out to the thickness of a small coin and cut small bases with a pastry cutter
3. Place them on small pie molds and on the paste put a small spoonful of frangipane cream or jams of your choice, provided it is not jelly
4. Cover with a few bands of paste
5. Bake for half an hour in the oven
6. Glaze with sugar and cochineal

title: Tendons de Bœuf à l'Allemande (Beef Tendons German Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Two or three pounds of beef breast
- A quarter of a large cabbage
- Bouillon
- Parsley
- Chives
- A clove of garlic
- Two cloves
- A bay leaf
- Thyme
- Basil
- Four large whole onions
- Four sausages
- Salt
- Coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Take two or three pounds of beef breast, cut it neatly into three or four equal pieces
2. Blanch them briefly in boiling water
3. Also blanch half a large cabbage for a good quarter hour
4. Put the beef breast to cook with a little bouillon, a bouquet of parsley, chives, a clove of garlic, two cloves, a bay leaf, a little thyme, and basil
5. After an hour add the cabbage cut into three pieces, well pressed and tied, with four large whole onions
6. When everything is almost cooked, add four sausages, a little salt and coarse pepper
7. Finish cooking so that little sauce remains
8. Drain the meat and vegetables and dry them of their fat with a cloth
9. Arrange the beef in the middle of the plate, the cabbages and onions around it, the sausages on top
10. Pass the sauce through a sieve and remove the fat
11. Serve over the ragout

title: Tendons de Poitrine à l'Allemande (Veal Breast Tendons German Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- veal breast tendons
- parsley

## Directions

1. Cut the tendons into pieces
2. Blanch briefly in boiling water
3. Place in a pot and cook the same way as a chicken fricassée
4. When ready to serve and the liaison is made, add a pinch of blanched finely chopped parsley

title: Tendons de Veau au Blanc (Veal Tendons in White Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- butter
- flour
- bouillon
- coarse pepper
- mushrooms
- bouquet garni
- small onions

## Directions

1. Cut your tendons either into oyster pieces or another way; blanch them and put them in cold water
2. When they are cold, trim them
3. Mix a quarteron of butter in a pan; sauté your tendons in it
4. When they are well browned in your butter, pour over them a spoonful and a half of flour; sauté everything in your pan so the flour mixes with your butter
5. Moisten your tendons abundantly with bouillon; add some coarse pepper, mushrooms, and a bouquet garni
6. When your tendons have boiled for two hours, add small onions that are well peeled and all the same size
7. When your onions are cooked, if the sauce is too thin, sauté your tendons, your small onions, your mushrooms, put them in another pan, and reduce your sauce until there is enough left to sauce your dish

title: Tendons de Veau au Vert-Pré (Veal Tendons with Green Herbs)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- veal breast
- butter
- parsley
- chives
- clove
- bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- sorrel
- egg yolks

## Directions

1. Cut the tendons from a veal breast into equal pieces the width of a finger
2. Blanch them briefly in boiling water
3. Place them in a pot with a piece of butter, a bouquet of parsley, chives, two cloves, bay leaf, thyme, and basil
4. Simmer until the tendons are cooked and almost no sauce remains
5. Take two handfuls of sorrel with stems, wash well, press to remove water, place in a mortar and pound very fine
6. Press very hard to extract at least a half-glass of juice, pass through a sieve
7. Use this juice to dilute three egg yolks
8. Pour this mixture into the tendons and thicken on the fire without boiling, like a chicken fricassée

title: Tendons de Veau au Vert-Pré (Veal Tendons with Herbs)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- veal breast
- butter
- bouquet of parsley
- chives
- two cloves
- one bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- bouillon
- sorrel

## Directions

1. Cut the tendons from the veal breast into equal pieces the width of a finger.
2. Blanch them briefly in boiling water.
3. Place them in a casserole with a piece of butter, a bouquet of parsley, chives, two cloves, one bay leaf, thyme, and basil.
4. Moisten with bouillon and cook on low heat until the tendons are cooked and almost no sauce remains.
5. Do not degrease, but moisten only; take two handfuls of sorrel, remove the stems, wash them well, and squeeze them dry.

title: Tendons de Veau aux Tomates (Veal Tendons with Tomato Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- tomato sauce

## Directions

1. Prepare whole tendons forming a semicircle, that is to say the tendons of two breasts which you will not cut
2. Cook them as those said à la poêle (pan-fried)
3. At the moment of serving, drain them, glaze them, and dress them on the plate so it figures the edge of a tart
4. Pour a tomato sauce in the middle

title: Tendons de Veau en Chartreuse (Veal Tendons Chartreuse)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- carrots
- turnips
- small onions
- lettuce
- green beans
- green peas
- salt water
- consommé
- butter
- espagnole sauce
- meat glaze

## Directions

1. Prepare and cut your tendons into oyster pieces; cook them as those said à la poêle (pan-fried)
2. Turn thirty carrots and thirty turnips of the same thickness and length, with the rounds the size of a liard
3. Cook your carrots separately and your turnips in consommé
4. Cook forty small onions, all the same size and well peeled, taking care they are not too cooked so they remain whole
5. Cook twenty lettuce
6. When your roots are cooked, cut them the size of a liard and arrange them in a miroton pattern in the bottom of your buttered mold; make a border of carrots, one of turnips, one of small onions, one of green beans, and small peas which you will blanch in salt water to keep them green; arrange them like pearls in your mold
7. When the bottom is well decorated, put your carrots and turnips in sticks around it, ensuring it is regular so your chartreuse has a nice appearance at the bottom
8. Over your decoration, drain your lettuce, cut them in two, and put them in your mold so your decoration has a solid body to hold it
9. When you have well placed lettuce in the interior of your mold, drain your tendons and put them over your lettuce, placing them so there are no voids in your mold; ten to twelve tendons are sufficient, or fewer if your mold is small
10. Fill it with lettuce and the rest of your vegetables; your mold must be completely full so that when you turn it over on the plate, your roots are well supported from inside
11. Take the cooking liquid from your roots; clarify it; pass it through fine linen and reduce it
12. Add half a spoonful of reduced espagnole sauce and an amount of meat glaze the size of a walnut
13. At the moment of serving, pour your chartreuse onto your plate; if there is some liquid, aspirate it with a straw; pour the reduction over it as sauce

title: Tendons de Veau Poêlés (Braised Veal Tendons)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal breast
- lard
- carrots
- onions
- parsley
- chives
- bay leaves
- cloves
- thyme
- bouillon or water
- salt

## Directions

1. Take a veal breast
2. Remove the meats that cover your tendons
3. When your tendons are uncovered, cut them very close to the rib bones
4. When they are separated from the ribs, cut the tender red bones that hold to your tendons
5. After separating the tendons from your veal breast, cut them into oysters, that is to say in flat and long form, or in thick pieces forming a square, or leave them whole
6. Have your tendons disgorge
7. Blanch them
8. Pare them so they have a neat and even form
9. Place lard in a casserole
10. Arrange your tendons on top and cover them with lard
11. Pour a cooking liquid over it
12. Your tendons must cook for three hours
13. Before removing them from cooking, pay attention to whether the larding needle enters easily into your tendons
14. If you do not have a cooking utensil, put lard in the bottom of your casserole
15. Place your tendons on top and cover them with lard
16. Then place the trimmings and rib bones from your veal breast on top, with two or three carrots, four onions, a bunch of parsley and chives, two bay leaves, two cloves, a little thyme
17. Moisten your tendons with bouillon or water
18. Add the appropriate amount of salt

title: Tendons de Veau à la Jardinière (Veal Tendons with Vegetables)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- lettuce
- small roots
- sugar
- coarse pepper
- lard
- bouillon

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your tendons as those said à la poêle (pan-fried)
2. At the moment of serving, drain them, dress them in crowns, glaze them, and put a border of lettuce around them
3. In the middle of your tendons, place roots turned into small sticks or olives, arranged in a pyramid
4. If you do not have sauce for your small roots, make a light roux that you will moisten with the cooking liquid from your tendons
5. Turn your roots into small sticks, blanch them, and cook them in your sauce
6. Add a small piece of sugar and some coarse pepper
7. Cook your lettuce between two strips of lard with roots, and moisten with bouillon

title: Tendons de Veau à la Jardinière (Veal Tendons with Vegetables)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- lettuces
- small root vegetables

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your tendons as for the braised version
2. At the moment of serving, drain them
3. Arrange them in crowns and glaze them
4. Place a border of lettuces around them
5. In the middle of your tendons, place root vegetables turned into small batons or olives, arranged in a pyramid

title: Tendons de Veau à la Pointe d'Asperges (Veal Tendons with Asparagus Tips)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- asparagus tips
- salt water
- velouté
- butter
- egg yolks
- flour
- coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Prepare your tendons as those said poêlés (pan-fried); your tendons must be whole so that it forms a well
2. At the moment of serving, drain them, glaze them, and dress them on the plate
3. Have asparagus tips from which you remove the leaves up to the button; cut them to a length of eight or ten lines
4. Blanch them in salt water until they are three-quarters cooked; drain them and put them in cold water so they keep their green color
5. Take reduced velouté and put your asparagus in it, making sure to drain them well; let them boil two or three times in your velouté
6. At the moment of serving, bind your ragout with two egg yolks; at the same instant add butter the size of half an egg; pour your bound ragout in the middle of your tendons
7. If you do not have velouté, when your asparagus are blanched, put half a quarteron of butter in a pan; sauté your asparagus in it; when you see they are well heated, put a spoonful of flour; sauté everything together so the flour mixes with the asparagus and butter
8. Take the cooking liquid from your tendons, pass it through a silk sieve, and moisten your asparagus tips with it
9. The sauce must be short, as it should only boil three or four times; then add a liaison of two egg yolks, and pour your ragout in the middle of your tendons; ensure it has good salt; add some coarse pepper

title: Tendons de Veau à la Purée de Champignons (Veal Tendons with Mushroom Purée)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- veal tendons
- mushroom purée

## Directions

1. Prepare your tendons as the preceding ones
2. At the moment of serving, drain them, glaze them, and put a mushroom purée in the middle

title: Terrine de Quenelles de Lapin à la Lareynière (Rabbit Quenelle Terrine Lareynière Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Rabbit forcemeat, slightly firmer than standard
- Butter, one-sixth less than in standard forcemeat
- Eight or ten truffles
- Kidneys
- Chicken crests
- Small pieces of veal
- Lamb sweetbreads
- Truffles
- Two spoonfuls of velouté
- One spoonful of game stock
- Half-bottle of dry Madeira wine
- Mushrooms, turned and sautéed
- Lemon juice and water
- Four egg yolks
- Butter, the size of an egg
- Salt and pepper

## Directions

1. Make forcemeat slightly firmer than the standard recipe by using one-sixth less butter
2. Poach quenelles with a spoon as you would the standard recipe
3. Drain the quenelles
4. Cut eight or ten truffles into small lardons
5. Insert the truffle lardons into the quenelles at equal distances and of the same size, arranging four rows on each quenelle
6. Once all quenelles are studded, place them in a casserole with kidneys, chicken crests, small pieces of veal, lamb sweetbreads, and truffles
7. Pour two spoonfuls of velouté and one spoonful of game stock into the casserole
8. Add half-bottle of dry Madeira wine
9. Add mushrooms that have been turned and sautéed in lemon juice and water
10. Reduce the sauce by half
11. Remove the mushrooms and set aside with the garnish
12. Make a liaison with four egg yolks and butter the size of an egg
13. Turn the liaison over the fire to thicken it, but do not let it boil for fear it will break
14. Strain the liaison through a sieve over the garnish, ensuring the garnish is well heated
15. Serve the ragout in a terrine
16. You may use an espagnole instead of velouté, working the sauce the same way
17. Cook the truffles in the sauce
18. Check that the seasoning is correct

title: Terrine de Quenelles de Lapin à la Lareynnière (Rabbit Quenelle Terrine Lareynnière Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Rabbit quenelle forcemeat
- Butter
- Truffles
- Cockscombs
- Calf kidneys
- Veal sweetbreads
- Lamb sweetbreads
- Truffles

## Directions

1. Make your forcemeat slightly firmer than before by putting one-sixth less butter in the forcemeat
2. Poach them with a spoon as before
3. Drain them
4. Cut eight or ten truffles into small lardons
5. Place them in your quenelles at equal distances and of the same thickness
6. Place four rows on each quenelle
7. When all are studded, place them in a casserole with kidneys and cockscombs, small veal sweetbreads, lamb sweetbreads, and truffles

title: Terrine à la Paysanne (Country Terrine)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Beef brisket, sliced thin
- Lean bacon, sliced thin
- Parsley, minced
- Chives, minced
- Fine spices
- Bay leaf
- Eau-de-vie
- Water

## Directions

1. Make a layer of beef in a terrine, then a layer of bacon, then a bit of seasoning
2. Repeat layers
3. At the end, add a spoonful of eau-de-vie and two spoonfuls of water
4. Cook over hot ashes like beef à la mode, having well sealed the terrine
5. When cooked, decrease if you judge it appropriate, and serve in the terrine

title: Timbales (Timbales)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- One litron of flour
- A little water
- Half a glass of olive oil
- Quarter pound of lard
- Two egg yolks
- A little salt
- Meat or fish ragout (cooked, cooled, and in short sauce)
- Optional: leftover ragouts

## Directions

1. Place one litron of flour on a table and make a well in the center.
2. Add a little water, half a glass of olive oil, a quarter pound of lard, two egg yolks, and a little salt.
3. Knead this dough until it is very firm.
4. Take two-thirds of the dough and beat it with a rolling pin to the thickness of a small coin.
5. Place this dough in a small saucepan and spread it in the bottom and around the sides, taking care not to pierce it and ensuring it takes the shape of the pan well.
6. Add any cooked meat or fish ragout you wish, provided it is cooked, cooled, and in short sauce (you can even use this method to disguise all sorts of ragouts that have already been served).
7. Beat the remaining dough to the same thickness.
8. Cover the meat or fish with this dough and moisten the edges to seal them together, as you would for a pie.
9. Pinch all the edges to seal them together.
10. Cook in the oven or on the coals by burying the saucepan in hot ashes with fire underneath a cover.
11. When the pastry of your timbale is cooked, carefully turn it upside down into the serving dish.
12. Make a hole in the middle and serve.

title: Timbales de Biscuits (Biscuit Timbales)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- six eggs
- sugar equal to the weight of six eggs
- flour equal to the weight of three eggs

## Directions

1. Take six eggs and as much sugar weighing the weight of three eggs of flour, which will provide you with enough to make six timbales the size of a good glass each, which will make a small entremes dish
2. Follow the same method as for the Savoy cake found previously, with the difference that it only takes half an hour for cooking and the oven should be a little gentler

title: Tongue à l'Écarlate (Scarlet Tongue)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- beef tongue
- two ounces of saltpetre
- thyme
- bay leaf
- basil
- peppercorns
- salt
- water

## Directions

1. Have a beef tongue
2. Pound two ounces of saltpetre
3. Rub the tongue well all over with the pounded saltpetre
4. Put it in a terrine with thyme, bay leaf, basil, and peppercorns
5. Put two large handfuls of salt in boiling water
6. When the salt is dissolved and the water is cold, pour it over the tongue and leave it in the brine for five or six days or more
7. If you have the time, before cooking it, soak it to disgorge for two hours
8. Blanch it before cooking

title: Topinambours à l'espagnole (Artichokes à l'Espagnole)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- topinambours
- pepper
- espagnole sauce
- bouillon

## Directions

1. Turn the topinambours, that is, remove the skin by cutting into the vegetable
2. Wash them well
3. Place them in a casserole with a little coarse pepper, four tablespoons of espagnole sauce, and one pot spoon of bouillon
4. Place on the fire and let them boil for three quarters of an hour
5. If the sauce is not reduced enough, remove the topinambours and reduce the sauce further
6. Arrange the topinambours on a platter or in an entremets casserole and pour the sauce over them

title: Tourte de Morue (Cod Pie)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- cod
- pastry
- butter
- coarse pepper
- bouquet garni
- cream sauce

## Directions

1. Cook cod in water, drain and let cool
2. Place the cod in flakes into the pastry with butter, coarse pepper, and a bouquet garni
3. When the pie is fully cooked, remove the bouquet and add a cream sauce like the previous recipe

title: Tourtes (Jam Tarts)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Puff pastry
- Jam or marmalade of your choice (apricot marmalade, cherry jam, verjuice jam, apple marmalade, etc.)
- Fine sugar
- Water

## Directions

1. Take puff pastry as described above, place it in the bottom of a tart pan
2. Put the jam you intend for the tart on the pastry, leaving a border of one inch that you will moisten with brushes dipped in water
3. Place small strips of pastry arranged in a design over the top, which will cover all the jam, and make a border of pastry for your tart
4. Bake in the oven; it should take at most one hour for cooking
5. When it is cooked, sprinkle fine sugar over it and pass the hot spatula over it to glaze it

title: Tourtes avec de la Gelée (Tarts with Jelly)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Puff pastry
- Fine sugar
- Jelly (redcurrant, raspberry, apple, quince, or cherry)

## Directions

1. Place puff pastry in the bottom of your tart pan
2. Put a border of pastry as with the others, and bake it without further preparation
3. When your pastry is cooked, put fine sugar on the border and glaze it with the hot spatula
4. As soon as the tart is cooled, cover the entire bottom of the tart up to the edge with the jelly you wish to put in it
5. Serve cold as an entremets

title: Tourtes de Côtelettes de Mouton à la Périgord (Mutton Chop Pie Périgord Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- a rack of mutton, cut into short chops with only the bone marking the chop
- medium truffles, peeled
- fine salt
- a little mixed spices
- lard strips
- butter the thickness of an ecu
- good coulis
- a good glass of Champagne wine, or good white wine if you do not have Champagne

## Directions

1. Line the pie dish with dough as described before
2. Arrange the chops on top
3. Place the peeled truffles between the chops
4. Season on top with fine salt and a little mixed spices
5. Cover with lard strips
6. Put butter the thickness of an ecu all over the lard strips
7. Finish the pie as described before
8. Bake for at least three hours
9. When cooked, add a good coulis to which you have added a good glass of Champagne wine, or good white wine if you do not have Champagne

title: Tourtes de Gibier (Game Pies)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Rabbit, hare, woodcock, or larks
- Lard
- Bouquet of fine herbs
- Salt
- Fine spices
- Butter
- Pastry dough
- Good coulis
- Juice of oranges
- Optional: veal sweetbreads with mushrooms or sliced truffles

## Directions

1. For rabbit: cut into pieces and crack the bones slightly with the back of a cleaver.
2. For hare: remove all bones and use only the meat; reserve bones for civet.
3. For woodcock: take two birds, cut each into four pieces, and lard the inside with lard before placing at the bottom of the pie.
4. For larks: remove the legs and head, empty the inside, and make a stuffing as you would for woodcock.
5. Place the game in the pie dish with a bouquet of fine herbs, salt, and fine spices.
6. Cover with lard and butter.
7. Cover with a layer of pastry dough and finish as for other pies.
8. When cooked and degreased, fill with a good sauce made from coulis.
9. When serving, press the juice of two oranges into the sauce.
10. Alternatively, use a good ragout of veal sweetbreads with mushrooms or sliced truffles.
11. Always add the juice of one orange when serving, as game benefits from a slightly tart flavor.

title: Tourtes de Toutes Sortes de Farces (Pies with Various Forcemeats)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Half a pound to three-quarters pound of meat (beef tenderloin, veal round, leg of mutton, game, or poultry)
- Beef fat (equal amount to the meat)
- Parsley
- Chives
- Mushrooms
- Salt
- Mixed spices
- Two whole eggs
- Half a septier of cream
- Pastry dough
- Good clear coulis

## Directions

1. Choose meat without small bones or tendons, removing any if found.
2. Mince the meat finely with chopping knives.
3. Mix with an equal amount of good beef fat, parsley, chives, and mushrooms, all chopped very fine.
4. Season with fine salt and a little mixed spices.
5. Mix everything well and add two whole eggs and moisten with half a septier of cream.
6. Taste and adjust seasoning to good flavor.
7. Line a pie dish with pastry dough.
8. Spread the forcemeat on top to a thickness of one inch.
9. Cover with pastry dough and finish as for other pies.
10. Cook for two hours; if using beef or mutton, cook longer.
11. When cooked and degreased, cut the forcemeat into small squares with a knife.
12. Top with good clear coulis and serve.

title: Tourtes Maigres en Poissons (Meatless Fish Pies)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Fish such as eel, pike, salmon, or tench
- Bouquet of fine herbs
- Fine salt
- Fine spices
- Good butter
- Pastry dough
- Flour
- Butter
- White wine
- Lean broth or warm water
- Mushrooms
- Fish roe
- Capers

## Directions

1. Choose any fish you prefer.
2. Scale and cut the fish into sections.
3. Line a pie dish with pastry dough as described for other pies.
4. Place the fish on top with a bouquet of fine herbs, fine salt, and fine spices.
5. Cover all the fish with good butter.
6. Cover with a layer of pastry dough and finish the pie as explained for previous recipes.
7. Bake for one and a half hours.
8. When cooked and degreased as for others, prepare a lean ragout.
9. Make a roux by browning a pinch of flour with a little butter.
10. When the roux is made, moisten with half a septier of white wine, lean broth or warm water.
11. Add mushrooms, a bouquet of fine herbs, and a little salt.
12. Boil the ragout for half an hour and degrease it.
13. If you have fish roe, boil briefly in water and cool in fresh water, then add to the ragout and boil for half a quarter-hour.
14. When finished and of good taste, pour into the pie.
15. You may also use various lean ragouts such as truffles, morels, mushrooms, or asparagus tips, depending on the season.

title: Tous les petits Oiseaux (All Small Birds)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- small birds
- butter
- fines herbes

## Directions

1. They are put on the spit like larks, or they are sautéed in butter and fines herbes

title: Truffes à la Maréchale (Truffles Marshal Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Beautiful truffles, well washed and brushed
- Salt
- Coarse pepper
- Paper

## Directions

1. Take beautiful truffles well washed and brushed
2. Season four truffles with salt and coarse pepper
3. Wrap them in several pieces of paper
4. Place in a small pot without any liquid
5. Cook in hot ashes for about one hour
6. Serve them hot in their natural state

title: Truite au court-bouillon (Trout in Court-Bouillon)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Trout
- Onions
- Four cloves
- Four bay leaves
- Sprigs of thyme
- Handful of parsley in branches
- Salt
- Six bottles of white wine

## Directions

1. Clean the trout without opening its belly
2. Wash and dry it well
3. Tie its head and put it in a fish kettle
4. Cut onions into slices, add four cloves, four bay leaves, and several sprigs of thyme
5. Add a handful of parsley in branches and salt
6. Add six bottles of white wine
7. Simmer for an hour, or longer if it is large
8. Serve on a napkin that you fold on a plate
9. Arrange parsley à la génoise around it
10. Moisten with red wine and add little salt so you can use its cooking liquid passed through a silk sieve

title: Truite Saumonée et Commune (Salmon Trout and Common Trout)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- salmon trout or common trout
- red wine
- parsley

## Directions

1. Cook the trout in a court-bouillon with red wine.
2. Serve on a napkin garnished with green parsley.
3. If serving as an entrée, serve with a sauce on top as for other fish.
4. Alternatively, soak in oil and cook on a grill as previously explained for other fish, and serve with a lean ragout.

title: Tuna (Thon)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Marinated tuna from Provence
- Good butter
- Parsley
- Chives (ciboules), chopped
- Breadcrumbs (mie de pain)

## Directions

1. Arrange the tuna on the serving dish
2. Serve with good butter, parsley, and chopped chives
3. Coat with breadcrumbs
4. Brown in the oven or under a pastry dome cover

title: Turbot and Barbue Cooked with Fat (Turbot and Brill Cooked in Fat)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- turbot or barbue
- good slices of veal
- salt
- pepper
- bouquet garni of all sorts of fine herbs
- lard
- glass of Champagne wine

## Directions

1. Put it in a turbot pan with good slices of veal, salt, pepper, and a bouquet garni of all sorts of fine herbs
2. Cover it everywhere with lard slices
3. Sweat on gentle fire
4. Add a glass of Champagne wine
5. When cooked, serve with different rich sauces or ragouts

title: Turbot au Court-bouillon (Turbot in Court-bouillon)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- one turbot
- water to fill a large pot
- one pound of salt
- twenty bay leaves
- one handful of thyme
- one large handful of parsley branches
- twenty chives
- ten onions cut into slices
- lemon juice
- two pints of milk (if lemon is not used)

## Directions

1. After washing the turbot well, remove the gills and entrails from its pouch without damaging it
2. Make an incision on the dark side near the head along the line
3. Uncover and remove a piece of three joints to give flexibility to the turbot so it does not crack while cooking
4. With a larding needle and string, secure the thick part of the head with the bone that holds the pouch
5. Pour water into a large pot, one pound of salt, twenty bay leaves, one handful of thyme, one large handful of parsley branches, twenty chives, ten onions cut into slices
6. Boil the court-bouillon for a quarter hour
7. Strain it through a sieve and let it rest until it is completely clear
8. Place the turbot in a turbot cooker
9. Rub the white side well with lemon juice; if you do not use lemon, add two pints of milk, and pour the court-bouillon over it
10. Place on the fire and let it simmer without boiling for one hour, or longer if the turbot is very large
11. Ten minutes before serving, remove it with its sheet and let it drain
12. Arrange a napkin on a platter and slide the turbot onto it
13. If there are any cracks, fill them with parsley leaves and place some around it
14. Take care to unbind it

title: Turkey Feet
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- turkey feet
- salt, seasoning

## Directions

1. Cook the feet in the coals like beef tongue, as described on page 29, with good seasoning
2. When cooked and cooled, dip them in the fat from their cooking
3. Coat them with bread crumbs and grill to a beautiful color
4. Serve dry as an entrée

title: Tête d'Agneau (Lamb's Head)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- lamb head
- lard for barding
- blanc (white cooking liquid)

## Directions

1. Debone your lamb head up to the eye
2. Remove the lower jaw
3. Cut it up to the eye
4. Let it disgorge and blanch for a good quarter hour
5. Refresh it
6. Dry it
7. Singe it
8. Cover it with a bard of lard
9. Put it to cook in a blanc
10. Two hours suffice for it to be cooked
11. At the moment of serving, drain it and remove the strings
12. Place it on your platter
13. It is eaten plain, or with a mixed ragout, or a pasqualine composed of the liver, lights, feet, rice, and mushrooms, all in blanc

title: Tête d'Agneau de Plusieurs Façons (Lamb Head Several Ways)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Two lamb heads with neck attached
- Bouillon
- Large bouquet garni
- Salt
- Pepper
- Roots
- Onions
- Verjuice berries or half a lemon
- Cooking liquid
- Three egg yolks
- Pinch of parsley

## Directions

1. Take two lamb heads with neck attached; remove the jaws and muzzle
2. Blanch and cook in a white braise as with veal ears (page 85)
3. Place in a pot with bouillon, a large bouquet garni, salt, pepper, roots, onions, verjuice berries or half a lemon cut in slices
4. Remove the skin and cook over low heat
5. When cooked, uncover the brains and arrange in the serving dish
6. Serve with sauce of choice, such as Spanish sauce, ravigote sauce, poivrade sauce, or green herb sauce
7. For a simpler preparation: take the cooking bouillon, being careful it is not too salty, dilute with three egg yolks and a pinch of chopped parsley, thicken over heat, and serve over the heads
8. Alternatively, serve with a ragout of ribs, a salpicon, or a ragout of truffles
9. Potages with lamb head are also made, prepared in white

title: Tête d'Agneau à la Bourgeoise Simplified (Lamb Head Bourgeoise Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Lamb head parts
- Bouillon from cooking
- Three egg yolks
- Pinch of chopped parsley

## Directions

1. Take the cooking bouillon and ensure it is not too salty
2. Dilute with three egg yolks and a pinch of chopped parsley
3. Thicken the sauce over the fire
4. Serve over the cooked lamb head parts

title: Tête de Veau au Naturel (Calf's Head Natural)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- scalded calf's head
- water for boiling and cooling
- blanc (cooking liquid)
- lemon
- lard barding
- tongue
- salt
- coarse pepper
- butter
- parsley
- vinegar
- fine pepper
- chives or shallots

## Directions

1. Take a scalded calf's head; bone it down to the eyes; remove the lower jaws and cut the upper jaw to the eye.
2. Soak your head for two or three hours to disgorge.
3. Boil water in a large pot and put the head in it.
4. Ensure it is well immersed in the boiling water so it does not blacken.
5. Skim your water well so it does not soil your head.
6. When it has boiled for half an hour, remove it from the pot and put it in a bucket of cold water.
7. Leave it there for half an hour to cool; then remove it from your bucket and dry it well.
8. Flame your head over a well-heated stove to burn any remaining hairs.
9. Dry it, remove the tongue, the white and hard skins that are inside the mouth.
10. Gather the skins together; truss the head with string so it appears whole.
11. Rub it with lemon and cover it with a barding of lard.
12. Put it to cook in a blanc with the tongue.
13. When your head is in your blanc, bring it to a boil, skim it, put a round of buttered paper over it, and let it boil gently; three hours suffice to cook it.
14. Test by pressing with your finger; if the flesh yields, your head is cooked.
15. At the moment of serving, remove it from your blanc, drain it, dress it on the platter.
16. Cut the skin on the skull with the point of your knife; open the skull by separating it in two; remove the two bones covering the brain and leave it uncovered.
17. Remove the hard skin from the tongue; split it in two lengthwise.
18. Powder it with fine salt and coarse pepper; dip it in butter; grill it; place it on the snout of the head.
19. Powder your head with finely chopped parsley and serve with an oil cruet, or heat vinegar in a pan with salt, fine pepper, chives or shallots, and put this sauce in a sauceboat.
20. This head is served to follow the soup course.

title: Tête de Veau en Tortue (Calf's Head Tortue Style)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- lard for barding
- fine white linen
- one bottle dry Madeira wine
- four spoonfuls of large Spanish sauce
- three spoonfuls of consommé
- ten cloves of hot chili pepper, well crushed
- veal quenelles
- calf's tongue
- rooster crests and kidneys
- small pieces of veal
- veal sweetbreads in pieces
- eight or ten hard-boiled egg yolks
- twelve egg ends (whites cut into small cup shapes)
- cornichons cut into sticks
- turned mushrooms
- crayfish
- nasturtium seeds preserved in vinegar

## Directions

1. Prepare your head in pieces as described in the previous recipe
2. Have a fine, white linen cloth, washed in clean water, laid on the table with lard bards
3. Take the pieces of head, tie them with string, place them on the lard bards, and cover them with more lard bards
4. Wrap the pieces with the linen cloth and tie both ends with string
5. Place this wrapped head in a braising pan or casserole and put a lid on top
6. Add one bottle of dry Madeira wine
7. Bring to a boil; when it reaches a boil, reduce to a gentle simmer
8. Put fire on your cover and let your head cook gently: three hours of cooking is sufficient; test with a larding needle
9. If the pieces are still too firm, cook longer
10. At the moment of serving, take both ends of your linen cloth, remove it from the casserole, untie it, drain your pieces and brain which are inside, and arrange them on the plate
11. Pour the ragout over them
12. For the ragout: take four or more spoonfuls of large Spanish sauce (the sauce should be plentiful) and put in a casserole
13. Pour in one bottle of dry Madeira wine, three spoonfuls of consommé, ten cloves of hot chili pepper well crushed
14. Reduce your sauce to half
15. When finished, add veal quenelles, tongue cut into pieces, rooster crests and kidneys, small pieces of veal, veal sweetbreads in pieces and other cooked garnishes
16. You can add eight or ten hard-boiled egg yolks, twelve egg ends (the whites with the tip cut off forming a small cup), cornichons cut into sticks, turned mushrooms, crayfish, nasturtium seeds preserved in vinegar
17. Ensure the ragout is very hot but does not boil
18. Pour it over the head arranged in a pyramid; the ragout must be well seasoned
19. If you do not have Spanish sauce, make a somewhat strong roux so your sauce is somewhat plentiful; moisten it with a little cooking liquid and Madeira wine
20. You could also use the cooking liquid in which your head was cooked; if nothing else, add the garnishes you have available, but always cornichons, hard-boiled eggs, quenelles and chili pepper

title: Tête de Veau Farcie (Stuffed Calf's Head)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Salt
- coarse pepper
- parsley
- minced shallot
- half a bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- two tablespoons of brandy
- three egg yolks
- three egg whites
- calf's tongue
- calf's eyes
- calf's jowls
- calf's head skin
- calf's ears
- half a septier of white wine
- twice as much bouillon
- bouquet of parsley
- chives
- one garlic clove
- three cloves
- carrots
- onions
- vinegar
- coulis

## Directions

1. Season with salt, coarse pepper, parsley, minced shallot, half a bay leaf, thyme and basil chopped as powder
2. Add two tablespoons of brandy
3. Bind this stuffing with three egg yolks and three beaten egg whites
4. Take the tongue, eyes from which you remove all the dark part, and jowls; clean everything well after blanching in boiling water, and cut them into fillets or large pieces
5. Mix them into your stuffing
6. Place the calf's head skin without blanching into a pot, ears underneath, and fill with your stuffing
7. Sew it up pleating it like a purse and tie it all around to restore its natural shape
8. Put it to cook in a vessel just its size with half a septier of white wine, twice as much bouillon, a bouquet of parsley, chives, one garlic clove, three cloves, carrots, onions, salt and pepper
9. Cook on low heat for three hours
10. When cooked, let it drain of its fat and dry it well with a cloth after removing the string
11. Strain part of its cooking liquid through a sieve
12. Add a little coulis if you have it, and add a dash of vinegar
13. Reduce it on the fire to the consistency of a sauce and serve over the calf's head

title: Tête de Veau farcie (Stuffed Calf's Head)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head
- godiveau (veal forcemeat)
- velouté sauce, reduced
- four spices (more than usual)
- parsley, minced
- shallots, minced
- lemon
- fine white linen
- lard bards

## Directions

1. Debone your entire calf's head; be careful not to puncture it, that is, avoid any holes caused by the knife
2. Be careful that the eyes remain attached to the head
3. When deboned, let it soak in cold water to disgorge
4. Blanch it so your head retains the same shape as if it had bones
5. Put it in cold water, dry it, then singe it
6. Make godiveau; when it is three-quarters moistened, add two spoonfuls of reduced velouté
7. Add somewhat more than the usual amount of four spices to this farce, along with minced parsley and shallots
8. Fill your head with this godiveau, reshaping it to its original form with a trussing needle and string
9. Sew it so it maintains its shape
10. When your head is fully prepared, rub it with lemon
11. Have a fine white linen cloth on which you place lard bards
12. Place your head on it and cover it with bards
13. Wrap it and tie both ends of your linen cloth; then truss your head

title: Tête de Veau frite (Fried Calf's Head)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head, cooked plain or otherwise
- marinade
- batter for frying

## Directions

1. When your calf's head is cooked plain or otherwise, cut it into medium pieces
2. Put it in a vessel and pour marinade over it
3. Ensure all your pieces are soaked in the seasoning
4. Make a frying batter
5. Drain your pieces of head and put them in your batter
6. Your frying oil should not be very hot when you add your pieces of head

title: Tête de Veau à la Detillères (Calf's Head à la Detillères)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- white calf's head
- water for disgorging and blanching
- vinegar
- blanc (cooking liquid)
- espagnole sauce

## Directions

1. Have a very white calf's head; bone it; soak it to disgorge as the previous one; blanch it likewise.
2. Remove the brain that remains in the skull; soak it to disgorge; remove the fibers and the first skin that covers it.
3. Blanch it in boiling water and a dash of vinegar; cook it in a small blanc; three-quarters of an hour of cooking suffices.
4. When your calf's head is well cooled, remove it from the water, dry it well, flame it as the previous one.
5. Cut it into pieces, leaving the eyes whole and the ears likewise.
6. Truss these pieces and cook them as previously described.
7. When your head is cooked, at the moment of serving, remove it from the blanc, drain it and untruss it.
8. Arrange your pieces on the platter.
9. Separate the brain and place it at both ends.
10. Detach the tongue; cut it into small squares the size of a die and put it in the sauce.
11. Take almost a full tablespoon of espagnole sauce in which you...

title: Tête de Veau à la Poulette (Calf's Head with Cream Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- calf's head pieces
- fine herbs
- butter
- flour
- bouillon
- salt
- coarse pepper
- two or three eggs for liaison
- lemon juice or vinegar

## Directions

1. Brown fine herbs in butter
2. Add a little flour
3. Moisten with bouillon; add a little salt and coarse pepper
4. Boil your sauce for a quarter hour
5. Put your pieces of head in it
6. Simmer gently for a moment so it is hot
7. At the moment of serving, blend in a liaison of two or three eggs, according to the size of your ragout
8. Turn your ragout until it is thickened; do not let it boil with the liaison in it, because it would curdle
9. At the moment of serving, pour in a squeeze of lemon juice or a dash of vinegar

title: Tête de Veau à la Sainte-Menehould (Calf's Head Sainte-Menehould Style)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Calf's head
- water
- bouquet of parsley
- chives
- two garlic cloves
- three cloves
- bay leaf
- thyme
- basil
- salt
- pepper
- butter
- one egg
- two pinches of nutmeg
- three egg yolks
- two tablespoons of vinegar
- half a glass of bouillon
- breadcrumbs
- piquant sauce

## Directions

1. Remove the jaws and cut the muzzle near the eyes
2. Place it in a pot with water and blanch like a pot-au-feu
3. Add a bouquet of parsley, chives, two garlic cloves, three cloves, a bay leaf, thyme and basil
4. Season with salt and pepper and boil until tender
5. Remove the bones that are on the brain, dress it on the serving platter
6. Make a sauce by placing butter and a piece of meat in a small pot with a beaten egg, two pinches of nutmeg, salt and pepper, three egg yolks and two tablespoons of vinegar
7. Blend the egg together and add half a glass of bouillon
8. Thicken the sauce on the fire so it is nicely thick
9. Spread sauce all over the head, coat with breadcrumbs
10. Sprinkle the breadcrumbs with a little butter
11. Brown in the oven or under an oven cover elevated enough so it does not touch the breadcrumbs
12. When it is a beautiful golden color, tilt the plat to drain the fat, wipe the edges
13. Serve a piquant sauce in the bottom

title: Veal Tendons au Soleil (Fried Breaded Veal Tendons)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal tendons
- Sauce from previous preparation
- Breadcrumbs
- Eggs
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Prepare and cook your tendons as in the blanc method
2. Bind them and place on a plate
3. Cover with their sauce and let cool
4. The sauce must be well seasoned
5. When cooled, bread them with breadcrumbs, then dip in egg
6. Pass them a second time through the coating
7. At the moment of serving, fry them; take care that your oil is not too hot
8. Put fried parsley on top

title: Veal Tendons en Marinade (Veal Tendons in Marinade)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal tendons, cut into oyster shapes
- Lard for bardes
- Clear marinade
- Batter for frying
- Parsley

## Directions

1. Cut your veal tendons into oyster shapes
2. Put lard bardes in the bottom of your pot, tendons on top
3. Cover them with lard
4. Moisten with clear marinade
5. Cook for two and a half hours
6. At the moment of serving, drain your tendons
7. Dip them in batter for frying
8. Take care that your oil is not too hot
9. Put a small handful of fried parsley on your fried tendons

title: Veal Tendons en Terrine (Veal Tendons in Terrine)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal tendons
- Flour
- Consommé
- Velouté (1 spoonful)
- Mushrooms
- Bouquet garni
- Pepper
- Veal sweetbreads
- Rooster crests and kidneys
- Veal threads, sautéed
- Chicken quenelles (12)
- Egg yolks (5 or 6)

## Directions

1. Prepare your tendons as in the blanc method
2. After adding flour, moisten with consommé
3. Add one spoonful of velouté
4. Add mushrooms, bouquet garni, and a little pepper
5. Cook the tendons for three hours
6. The sauce must be abundant as there will be garnishes in the terrine: small veal sweetbreads, rooster crests and kidneys, sautéed veal threads, and twelve chicken quenelles, all cooked separately
7. At the moment of serving, drain the garnishes and place in the terrine
8. Place the tendons and mushrooms in the terrine
9. Bind the sauce with five or six egg yolks according to how thin the sauce is
10. Pass it through a sieve over the tendons

title: Veal Tendons with Mushrooms (Tendons de Veau à la Bourgeoise)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal tendons
- Butter
- Flour (1.5 spoonfuls)
- Bouillon
- Pepper
- Mushrooms
- Bouquet garni
- Small onions
- Egg yolks (3)

## Directions

1. When the tendons are well browned in your butter, pour over them one and a half spoonfuls of flour
2. Sauté everything in your pot: to mix the flour with your butter, moisten the tendons well with bouillon
3. Add a little pepper, mushrooms, and a bouquet garni
4. When the tendons have boiled for two hours, add small onions that are well peeled and all the same size
5. When the onions are cooked, if the sauce is too thin, remove the tendons, onions, and mushrooms and place them in another pot, and reduce the sauce until there is enough to coat the tendons
6. Pass the sauce through a sieve over the tendons; keep it hot in a bain-marie
7. At the moment of serving, bind with a liaison of three egg yolks
8. Arrange the tendons on the plate with the onions and mushrooms on top

title: Veal Tendons with Peas (Tendons de Veau aux Petits Pois)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Veal tendons
- Fine peas (size of half a walnut)
- Eggs (3 yolks)
- Roux (if browning desired)
- Bouillon or water
- Parsley and chives
- Bay leaf
- Salt
- Pepper

## Directions

1. Prepare your tendons as in the blanc method
2. When the tendons are half cooked, add your fine peas, sized like half a walnut
3. At the moment of serving, add a liaison of three eggs
4. If you want your ragoût to be brown, make a roux and pass your tendons through it
5. When they are well coated, moisten with bouillon; if you have none, use water
6. Add a bunch of parsley and chives, a bay leaf, salt, and pepper
7. When your tendons are half cooked, add your peas, sized like half a walnut
8. When your ragoût is finished, make sure to degrease it
9. Check that it has a good flavor

title: Velouté (Simplified Veal Velouté)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 3 or 4 pounds of veal trimmings, either neck, breast, shoulder, leg, shank, cutlet trimmings or poultry scraps
- a few carrots
- onions
- bouquet of parsley and chives
- 3 bay leaves
- 3 cloves
- water, one pot spoon full (or broth if available)
- salt to taste
- white roux
- mushrooms or mushroom trimmings

## Directions

1. Place 3 or 4 pounds of meat trimmings in a casserole with a few carrots, onions, bouquet of parsley and chives, 3 bay leaves, and 3 cloves
2. Add one pot spoon full of water (lacking broth)
3. Place your casserole with your meat on a fairly hot fire
4. Skim your moisture well, and when it is reduced, prevent it from sticking
5. Fill your casserole almost full with water if you do not have broth
6. Add the appropriate salt
7. Let your sauce boil, then skim it, then place it on the edge of the stove so it simmers for 2 hours
8. When your meat is cooked, pass this moisture through a silk sieve
9. Make a white roux, when it reaches its point, add mushrooms or mushroom trimmings which you will stir for ten minutes in your white roux
10. Then pour the moisture in which your meat cooked into it
11. Be careful to dilute your roux little by little so it does not become lumpy
12. When you have added everything, let your velouté boil
13. Skim it and place it on the edge of the stove so it simmers for an hour and a half
14. Degrease it and pass it through cheesecloth
15. Be careful to avoid color in your velouté; the whitest is the most beautiful

title: Velouté (Veal Velouté Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 or 3 pieces of veal knuckle from the leg
- 2 chickens
- 4 carrots
- 4 onions, one studded with 2 cloves
- a large bouquet of parsley and chives
- consommé, one pot spoon full
- white roux
- about 20 mushrooms, sautéed cold in water and lemon

## Directions

1. Place all the meat and vegetables into a casserole
2. Add one pot spoon full of consommé
3. Place your casserole on a fairly hot fire
4. Be careful to skim your meats well and wipe the interior of your casserole so your sauce is not cloudy
5. When you see your moisture is diminished and forms large bubbles when boiling, moisten your sauce with consommé
6. Ensure it is very clear and has no brown color
7. When you have filled your casserole with consommé, skim it well
8. When it boils, place it on the corner of the stove
9. Make a white roux in which you place about 20 mushrooms that you have sautéed cold in water and lemon, which you will stir in your hot roux
10. Dilute your white roux with the moisture from your velouté
11. Pour it over your meats
12. Make your sauce boil on the corner of the stove
13. Skim it well
14. After an hour and a half, degrease it
15. When your meat is cooked, pass your sauce through cheesecloth
16. Ensure your velouté is as white as possible

title: Velouté (Velouté Sauce)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- consommé
- mushrooms

## Directions

1. Prepare the velouté as you would the Spanish sauce
2. Reduce, degrease, and skim in the same manner
3. Ensure your velouté remains very white; for this avoid using consommé that has color
4. Add mushrooms
5. Strain through a sieve
6. Keep warm in a bain-marie

title: Velouté Maigre (Lean Velouté)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- fish and vegetables (as for lean brown sauce)
- two handfuls of mushrooms
- white roux
- silk sieve

## Directions

1. Prepare your fish and vegetables as for the brown sauce
2. Put them in your saucepan
3. Make the velouté, but do not let it take color
4. When you see there is no more liquid in your saucepan, use the same procedure as the previous sauce
5. When your fish has boiled for an hour and a half, pass your liquid through a silk sieve
6. Make a white roux
7. When it reaches the right point, put two handfuls of mushrooms into it and stir them in your white roux
8. Pour the fish juice into it and stir well to avoid lumps
9. Let the velouté boil for an hour, skim and degrease it
10. Pass it through cheesecloth, ensuring it is neither too thick nor too thin, and that your sauce has good seasoning

title: Vermicelle au Lait (Vermicelli Milk Soup)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- milk
- vermicelli
- sugar
- salt

## Directions

1. When your milk boils, you put your vermicelli in it; be very careful to separate your vermicelli; when it is in your milk, you must stir it from time to time, so that it does not turn into paste; that your soup be of good sugar: half an hour suffices for your vermicelli to burst.

title: Vermicelle au Lait d'Amandes (Vermicelli with Almond Milk)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- vermicelli
- almond milk
- sugar
- salt

## Directions

1. You will make your vermicelli as the previous one; at the moment of serving you will pour an almond milk into it; that your soup be very hot, of good sugar, with a little salt.

title: Vermicelle Faubonne (Vermicelli Faubonne)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Root vegetables
- Vermicelli
- Bouillon
- Salt
- Coarse pepper

## Directions

1. Cut the root vegetables into small fillets instead of dice
2. Cook your vegetables before adding the vermicelli
3. Once the vegetables are cooked, put your vermicelli into your bouillon
4. Take care to skim from time to time so that your vermicelli does not clump together
5. Season with salt and coarse pepper

title: Vespetro (Royal Medicinal Liqueur)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- 2 pints of good eau-de-vie
- 2 gros of angelica seeds
- 1 ounce of coriander seeds
- 1 good pinch of fennel
- as much anise
- juice of 2 lemons with the zests of the peels
- 1 pound of sugar

## Directions

1. Coarsely crush the seeds in a mortar
2. Put the eau-de-vie in a large glass or stoneware bottle
3. Add the crushed seeds, lemon juice with zests, and sugar
4. Let infuse in the bottle for four or five days
5. Stir the bottle from time to time to dissolve the sugar
6. Pass the liqueur through cotton or gray paper to make it clearer
7. Put it in bottles and cork them well

title: Vieilles Perdrix (Old Partridges)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Old partridges
- White wine
- Lentil coulis
- Bacon (petit lard)

## Directions

1. Always cook them braised, as you would beef tongue (page 29), adding white wine
2. When cooked, place them in a terrine with lentil coulis and bacon

title: Vin de Cerises (Cherry Wine)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Fifteen pounds of cherries
- Two pounds of currants
- Two-thirds of the pits from the cherries

## Directions

1. To make five pints of cherry wine, take fifteen pounds of cherries with two pounds of currants that you crush well together
2. Crush two-thirds of the pits and mix them together
3. Put everything in a barrel

title: Vin de Cerises (Cherry Wine)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- 15 pounds of cherries
- 2 pounds of currants
- two-thirds of the pits, crushed
- 1 quarteron of sugar per pint of juice

## Directions

1. Crush the cherries and currants together well
2. Crush two-thirds of the pits and mix them together
3. Put everything in a barrel with a quarteron of sugar per pint of juice
4. Ensure the barrel is full and cover it only with a grape leaf and sand around it
5. Let it ferment for about three weeks until it stops bubbling
6. Keep the barrel always full by adding cherry juice as needed
7. Once it stops bubbling, cork it with a good stopper
8. After two months, draw it off clear and put it in bottles

title: Vinaigre Printannier (Spring Vinegar)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- All sorts of small herbs, such as cress, tarragon, salad burnet, and chervil
- Ten cloves of garlic
- As many shallots
- Six onions
- A handful of mustard seed
- Twenty cloves
- A half-gros of mace
- One gros of long pepper
- One lemon cut into slices with its peel
- Vinegar

## Directions

1. In spring, around the month of June, gather all sorts of small herbs such as cress, tarragon, salad burnet, and chervil.
2. Dry these small herbs in the sun.
3. When they are dry, put them in a jug of about six pints capacity, with ten cloves of garlic, as many shallots, six onions, a handful of mustard seed, twenty cloves, a half-gros of mace, one gros of long pepper, and one lemon cut into slices with its peel.
4. Fill the jug with vinegar; after having well stopped it, expose it for eight days in the heat of the sun.
5. Pass it through a strainer to clarify it, put it in bottles that you will well stop, and you will use it afterwards as you judge fit.

title: Weever (Vive)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Weever fish
- Oil
- Salt
- Pepper
- Butter
- Capers
- Anchovies
- Flour
- Water

## Directions

1. Scale, gut, wash and dry the weever thoroughly
2. Cut lightly in five or six places on each side
3. Soak with a little oil, salt, and pepper
4. Grill and baste from time to time with the remaining oil
5. Prepare sauce with butter, capers, anchovies, a little flour and water, salt and pepper
6. Thicken the sauce on the fire and serve over the weever

title: Whiting (Merlans)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Whiting
- Flour
- Oil or fat for frying

## Directions

1. Scale, gut, wash and dry the whiting
2. Keep the livers in the body
3. Cut lightly in five or six places on each side
4. Dredge in flour
5. Fry at high heat
6. Serve on a napkin as a roast dish

title: Whiting as an Entrée (Merlans en Entrée)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- Whiting
- Flour
- Oil or fat for frying
- Butter
- Capers
- Anchovies

## Directions

1. Prepare and fry the whiting as above
2. Remove the head and center bone
3. Arrange the fillets on the serving dish with the white side up
4. Put a white sauce with capers and anchovies on top

title: Wings and Thighs of Goose Preserved in Fat
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- geese as desired
- goose fat from cooking
- lard
- bay leaves
- salt

## Directions

1. Scald and clean the geese and place them on the spit to cook only three-quarters of the way through
2. Set aside the fat that renders during cooking
3. Let the geese cool, cut them in four by removing the thighs and keeping the breast with the wings
4. Arrange them tightly packed in a pot of fat, placing three or four bay leaves and salt between each layer
5. Melt the goose fat that was set aside with much lard; there must be enough to cover the wings and thighs completely
6. Place this fat in the pot and cover with parchment only twenty-four hours after everything is completely cold
7. Store them in a dry place
8. When you wish to use them, remove them from the pot and their fat as needed, wash them in hot water before using them as desired

title: Wings en Fricandeau (Turkey or Hen Wings)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- ten to twelve turkey wings or fifteen hen wings
- small lardons
- water

## Directions

1. Scald and pluck the wings
2. Lard them with small lardons
3. Blanch them briefly in boiling water
4. Cook them as fricandeaux à la bourgeoise, as described on page 118

title: Wings en Matelotte (Wings in Wine Sauce)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- one spoonful of flour and butter
- one half-septier of white wine
- one half-septier of good bouillon
- wings
- parsley, chives, two cloves of garlic, thyme, basil, two cloves, salt, coarse pepper
- at least a dozen small white onions, blanched
- bread crumbs the size of a small coin
- butter
- a pinch of fine capers

## Directions

1. Make a roux with flour and butter, moisten with white wine and bouillon
2. Put in the wings with herbs and seasonings
3. At half cooking time, add the small onions that have been blanched for a quarter hour and cleaned
4. Cut bread into small pieces and brown them in butter
5. When the ragout is finished with reduced sauce, add the capers
6. Arrange the wings and croutons on a platter and pour sauce over

title: Wings in Champagne Wine
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- veal slices
- wings
- lard strips
- bouquet garni
- salt, coarse pepper
- one glass of Champagne wine
- one half-glass of good bouillon
- two spoonfuls of coulis

## Directions

1. Line a casserole with veal slices
2. Place wings on top and cover with lard strips
3. Add bouquet garni, salt, coarse pepper, Champagne wine, and bouillon
4. Cook gently until done
5. Add coulis to the sauce, degrease it, and pass through a sieve
6. Serve over the wings, wiped of excess fat

title: Wings with Green Purée
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- wings
- good bouillon
- bouquet garni
- salt, pepper
- small lard
- half-litre of peas cooked with bouillon
- chive stalks and parsley
- étamine cloth

## Directions

1. Cook wings with bouillon, bouquet garni, salt, pepper, and small lard
2. Cook peas with bouillon, chive stalks, and parsley
3. Pass the peas through an étamine cloth as a purée
4. Add the cooking liquid from the wings to the purée for flavor
5. Serve the purée over the wings and small lard in a terrine
6. The purée should be neither too thin nor too thick

title: Wings with Lentil Purée
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- wings
- lentil purée

## Directions

1. Prepare the same way as wings with green purée, except do not add chive stalks or parsley to the lentil cooking

title: Wings with Small Onions
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- wings
- bouillon
- bouquet garni
- salt, coarse pepper
- small onions ragout

## Directions

1. Make a small onion ragout as explained elsewhere
2. Cook the wings in a casserole with bouillon, bouquet garni, salt, and coarse pepper
3. Degrease the cooking liquid and pass it through a sieve
4. Add it to the onion ragout to strengthen it
5. Serve with reduced sauce over the onions

title: Wings à l'Espagnole (Spanish-Style Wings)
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- wings, scalded and plucked
- lard strips
- two spoonfuls of oil
- one glass of white wine
- one glass of good bouillon
- parsley, chives, two cloves of garlic, thyme, bay leaf, basil, two cloves, salt, coarse pepper, two pinches of coriander
- coulis

## Directions

1. Place wings in a casserole over lard strips with oil, white wine, bouillon, and herbs
2. Cook until done
3. Pass the sauce through a sieve and degrease it
4. Add a little coulis to bind the sauce
5. Wipe the wings with a white cloth
6. Serve the sauce over the wings

title: Wings à la Sainte-Menehould
author: Menon
body:
## Ingredients

- wings
- one glass of bouillon
- one half-glass of white wine
- bouquet garni
- salt, coarse pepper
- oil
- bread crumbs

## Directions

1. Cook wings gently with bouillon, white wine, bouquet garni, salt, and coarse pepper
2. When cooked, reduce all the sauce onto the wings as if glazing them
3. Cool and dip in a little oil
4. Coat with bread crumbs
5. Drizzle with a little oil and grill to a beautiful color
6. Serve without sauce or with a clear well-seasoned sauce

title: Young Partridges with Truffles (Perdreaux aux Truffes)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- three large young partridges
- lard
- medium truffles
- salt
- coarse pepper
- quatre épices (four spices)
- barding lard
- butter
- espagnole sauce
- game stock
- flour

## Directions

1. Take three large young partridges and clean through the breast
2. Singe lightly, taking care not to damage them
3. Pound or mince a pound of lard and place in a casserole
4. Add medium truffles cut into quarters
5. Round the pieces, chop them, and pass through heat
6. Add a little salt, coarse pepper, and four spices
7. When you simmer them with the lard for ten minutes, add the chopped truffle pieces
8. Remove from heat and let cool
9. Stuff the partridges with this mixture
10. Tie them, giving them a well-rounded shape
11. Arrange bards of lard in a casserole with the partridges on top
12. Cover with bards
13. Make a braising pan (poêle) without lemon and place on top
14. Simmer for an hour and a half
15. At service, drain, untie, and dress on a platter
16. Chop two truffles and pass through a little butter
17. Add three tablespoons of espagnole and three tablespoons of game stock
18. Reduce sauce by half, degrease, and serve with the partridges
19. If you do not have espagnole sauce, pass the chopped truffles with a half spoonful of flour and the cooking liquid through a silk sieve
20. Reduce the sauce by half, degrease, and serve under the partridges
21. They may also be roasted on a spit

title: Young Partridges à l'Espagnole (Perdreaux à l'Espagnole)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- three young partridges of equal size
- butter
- lemon juice
- coarse pepper
- ham slice
- espagnole sauce
- white wine
- bay leaf
- parsley and chives
- clove

## Directions

1. Pluck and clean three young partridges of the same size
2. Singe lightly
3. Truss and tie them as for a braised hen
4. Put a quarter pound of butter in a casserole with the juice of a lemon, a little coarse pepper, and a slice of ham
5. Place the partridges on a moderate heat
6. Make them turn gently so they do not brown
7. When they are well turned, add six tablespoons of espagnole, half a bottle of white wine, a bay leaf, a bouquet of parsley and chives, and a clove
8. Simmer for three-quarters of an hour
9. Remove when cooked and place in a casserole
10. Degrease the sauce and reduce by half
11. Pass the sauce through cheesecloth over the partridges
12. At service, untie them and dress on a platter
13. Serve the sauce under them

title: Échine de Cochon (Pork Backbone)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- pork backbone
- fat

## Directions

1. Cut your piece very squarely
2. Leave everywhere the thickness of a finger of fat; your square must be well covered
3. Score the top, that is to say the fat that covers it
4. Spit it for two hours
5. Before serving, put it on the fire
6. Serve it as a roast or as an entrée, with a Sauce Robert, a tomato sauce or a piquant sauce

title: Échine de Cochon (Pork Loin)
author: A. Viard
body:
## Ingredients

- Pork loin
- Sauce Robert, tomato sauce, or piquant sauce

## Directions

1. Cut your piece of meat squarely.
2. Leave everywhere the thickness of one finger of fat; your square piece should be well covered.
3. Slash the top, that is to say the fat that covers it.
4. Spit it for two hours.
5. Before serving, put it on the fire.
6. Serve it as a roast or as an entrée with a sauce Robert, a tomato sauce, or a piquant sauce.

title: Écrevisses (Crayfish)
